It is toxic to skin and mucous membranes, mutagenic, and requires proper ventilation and PPE. It is NOT a sterilant at short exposure times. Alcohol is not sporicidal and not used for endoscopes. Hypochlorite is corrosive to metal instruments.
highYield: 2% Glutaraldehyde: HLD in 20-30 min; sterilization in 10 hours; endoscopes; mutagenic/irritant; alkaline-activated; broad-spectrum including mycobacteria.
pyqNote: Multi-feature question on glutaraldehyde. Integrates disinfection level, time, toxicity, and use. A complex NEET PG or AIIMS PG style question.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 45-year-old male with chronic hepatitis B presents with a 3-month history of right upper quadrant pain and weight loss. Ultrasound reveals a 4 cm hepatic mass. Which tumor marker is MOST elevated and specific for this diagnosis?
explanation: AFP is the tumor marker of choice for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). It is elevated in >70% of HCC cases. AFP is also physiologically elevated in fetal serum (synthesized by fetal liver and yolk sac). Levels >400 ng/mL in the setting of cirrhosis and a hepatic mass are diagnostic of HCC without biopsy. CA 19-9 is for pancreatic cancer, CEA for colorectal cancer, and CA-125 for ovarian cancer.
highYield: AFP is the hallmark marker for HCC, especially in a patient with chronic hepatitis B/C or cirrhosis. Also remember AFP is elevated in yolk sac (endodermal sinus) tumor of the testis/ovary.
pyqNote: NEET PG/DNB frequently asks about the tumor marker for HCC in the context of chronic viral hepatitis. AFP >400 ng/mL + liver mass = HCC diagnosis.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 22-year-old male presents with a painless testicular mass. Serum AFP is markedly elevated but beta-hCG is normal. Histology shows a tumor with Schiller-Duval bodies. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: Yolk sac tumor (endodermal sinus tumor) is the most common testicular tumor in children and is characterized by markedly elevated AFP and the presence of Schiller-Duval bodies (glomeruloid structures resembling fetal glomeruli). Choriocarcinoma markedly elevates beta-hCG, NOT AFP. Seminoma may elevate PLAP and LDH but not AFP. Embryonal carcinoma can elevate both AFP and hCG but lacks Schiller-Duval bodies.
highYield: Schiller-Duval bodies are pathognomonic of yolk sac tumor. AFP is the marker. Choriocarcinoma = beta-hCG elevated, AFP NOT elevated — a classic NEET PG distinction.
pyqNote: This combination (Schiller-Duval bodies + high AFP + low hCG) has appeared multiple times in NEET PG and AIIMS PG exams.

subject: Pathology
stem: A patient with gestational trophoblastic disease is found to have markedly elevated serum beta-hCG with no elevation of AFP. Histology shows cytotrophoblasts and syncytiotrophoblasts without chorionic villi. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Choriocarcinoma is characterized by: (1) markedly elevated beta-hCG (produced by syncytiotrophoblasts), (2) absence of chorionic villi on histology, (3) bimodal cytotrophoblast and syncytiotrophoblast pattern. Critically, AFP is NOT elevated in choriocarcinoma — only beta-hCG. Hydatidiform moles have chorionic villi. Placental site trophoblastic tumor secretes HPL (human placental lactogen), not hCG predominantly.
highYield: CRITICAL: Choriocarcinoma elevates beta-hCG but NOT AFP. AFP is elevated in yolk sac tumor and HCC. Do not confuse these.
pyqNote: The distinction between AFP (yolk sac tumor) vs beta-hCG (choriocarcinoma) is a perennial NEET PG/AIIMS favorite.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 60-year-old male undergoes surgical resection of colorectal carcinoma. Post-operatively, his CEA level falls to normal. Six months later, there is a rising CEA with no symptoms. What is the BEST clinical use of CEA in this scenario?
options: Screening for colorectal cancer in general population
Confirming the diagnosis of colorectal cancer
explanation: CEA (Carcinoembryonic antigen) is BEST used for monitoring recurrence after curative resection of colorectal cancer. A rising CEA after successful surgery suggests recurrence (often hepatic metastasis). CEA is NOT suitable for screening (low specificity — elevated in smokers, liver disease, IBD, pancreatitis) and NOT for diagnosis (non-specific). It is elevated in colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, lung, and breast cancers.
highYield: CEA = monitoring recurrence of colorectal cancer, NOT diagnosis or screening. Also elevated in smokers (important NEET PG trap).
pyqNote: NEET PG repeatedly tests the clinical utility of CEA — the answer is always 'monitoring recurrence,' not diagnosis or screening.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of endometriosis presents with a pelvic mass. CA-125 is elevated to 180 U/mL. What is the CORRECT interpretation?
options: CA-125 is specific for ovarian cancer and excludes benign disease
CA-125 can be elevated in endometriosis, PID, and peritoneal disease — it has poor specificity
CA-125 is the best screening test for ovarian cancer in general population
explanation: CA-125 has POOR SPECIFICITY. It is elevated in: ovarian serous carcinoma (best utility here), but ALSO in endometriosis, PID, peritonitis, pleural effusion, liver cirrhosis, and first trimester pregnancy. It cannot differentiate benign from malignant disease. It is most useful for monitoring response to chemotherapy in known serous ovarian carcinoma. It is NOT recommended for population screening due to high false positive rate.
highYield: CA-125 = ovarian serous carcinoma marker (especially type II high-grade), but poor specificity. Elevated in endometriosis and PID — a classic NEET PG distractor.
pyqNote: Questions often present endometriosis or PID with elevated CA-125 to test whether students know CA-125 lacks specificity.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 58-year-old male presents with obstructive jaundice, weight loss, and epigastric pain. CT scan shows a pancreatic head mass. Which tumor marker is MOST useful, and what is an important caveat about false-negative results?
options: AFP — false negative in patients with hemolysis
CA 19-9 — false negative in patients who are Lewis antigen negative (Le a-b- blood group)
CA-125 — false negative in postmenopausal women
explanation: CA 19-9 (Sialyl-Lewis antigen) is the BEST tumor marker for pancreatic cancer (sensitivity ~70-80%). CRITICAL CAVEAT: CA 19-9 is a Lewis blood group antigen. Patients who are Lewis antigen negative (Le a-b-, approximately 5-10% of the population) cannot synthesize CA 19-9 and will always have a FALSE NEGATIVE result regardless of tumor burden. CA 19-9 is also elevated in cholangiocarcinoma and gastric cancer.
highYield: CA 19-9 = pancreatic cancer marker. False negatives occur in Lewis antigen-negative patients. This fact is a favorite high-yield NEET PG question.
pyqNote: The Lewis antigen-negative false negative for CA 19-9 is a classic NEET PG and AIIMS PG exam question.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 70-year-old male presents with urinary hesitancy, nocturia, and a PSA of 8 ng/mL. Digital rectal examination shows a uniformly enlarged, smooth prostate. What does this PSA result indicate?
options: PSA elevated = prostate cancer until proven otherwise
PSA is prostate-specific but NOT prostate cancer-specific; can be elevated in BPH and prostatitis
PSA >4 ng/mL is diagnostic of prostate cancer
PSA is not useful in males over 65 years
explanation: PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen) is ORGAN-SPECIFIC (prostate) but NOT DISEASE-SPECIFIC (not prostate cancer-specific). PSA can be elevated in BPH, prostatitis, prostatic infarction, and after digital rectal examination/prostate biopsy/catheterization. A smooth, uniformly enlarged prostate in a 70-year-old man with LUTS suggests BPH, not cancer. PSA >4 ng/mL requires further evaluation but is NOT diagnostic. Free:total PSA ratio: lower ratio (<10-15%) = higher risk of malignancy.
highYield: PSA = prostate-specific (organ) NOT cancer-specific (disease). BPH and prostatitis also elevate PSA. Free:total PSA ratio is inversely related to cancer risk.
pyqNote: NEET PG commonly tests the distinction between PSA being prostate-specific vs prostate cancer-specific. The smooth prostate in BPH is a classic distractor.

subject: Pathology
stem: In prostate cancer evaluation, which statement about the free:total PSA ratio is CORRECT?
options: Higher free:total PSA ratio suggests higher risk of malignancy
Lower free:total PSA ratio (<15%) suggests higher risk of malignancy
Free PSA is higher in malignancy than BPH
Free:total PSA ratio is not clinically useful
explanation: In prostate cancer, PSA circulates predominantly in complexed (bound) form, resulting in a LOWER free:total PSA ratio (<10-15%). In BPH, more PSA is in free form, giving a HIGHER free:total ratio (>25%). Therefore: Lower free:total PSA ratio = more likely malignancy. This ratio is particularly useful in the gray zone (PSA 4-10 ng/mL). Gleason grading (2-10) assesses histological differentiation; higher Gleason = worse prognosis.
highYield: Lower free:total PSA ratio = higher cancer risk. This is frequently tested. In BPH: high free PSA. In cancer: low free PSA (more complexed).
pyqNote: Free:total PSA ratio question appears in NEET PG and AIIMS PG in the 'PSA gray zone' context (4-10 ng/mL).

subject: Pathology
stem: A 28-year-old male with a testicular tumor has elevated LDH and normal AFP and beta-hCG. What is the MOST likely tumor type and what does LDH elevation indicate?
options: Yolk sac tumor — LDH indicates yolk sac cell activity
Seminoma — LDH is a marker of tumor burden and prognosis in seminoma
Choriocarcinoma — LDH replaces beta-hCG in this tumor type
Embryonal carcinoma — LDH is the primary marker
explanation: Seminoma is the most common testicular germ cell tumor in adults. Seminomas do NOT elevate AFP (AFP elevation excludes pure seminoma) and have low/no beta-hCG elevation. LDH is the PRIMARY tumor marker for seminoma and serves as a marker of tumor burden and prognosis. LDH is also elevated in lymphoma, Ewing sarcoma, and non-specifically in hemolysis. PLAP (Placental Alkaline Phosphatase) is another marker for seminoma.
highYield: Seminoma markers: LDH + PLAP. AFP elevation EXCLUDES pure seminoma. LDH = tumor bulk and prognosis in seminoma and lymphoma.
pyqNote: The triad of testicular tumor + high LDH + normal AFP = seminoma is a standard NEET PG question. AFP positivity mandates reclassification as non-seminomatous.

subject: Pathology
stem: A tumor marker for seminoma is also elevated in heavy smokers. Which marker is this, and why is smoking relevant?
options: AFP — hepatocyte regeneration from smoking-related liver injury
CEA — non-specific elevation from mucosal irritation
PLAP (Placental Alkaline Phosphatase) — ectopic expression induced by hydrocarbons in tobacco smoke
LDH — muscle breakdown from chronic cough
explanation: PLAP (Placental Alkaline Phosphatase) is elevated in seminoma AND in heavy smokers (due to ectopic induction of PLAP by polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in tobacco smoke). This makes PLAP less specific in a smoking patient. CEA is also elevated in smokers. PLAP is an isoenzyme of alkaline phosphatase expressed ectopically in seminoma and placenta, hence the name.
highYield: PLAP = seminoma marker AND elevated in smokers (like CEA). Double-marker — both PLAP and CEA are falsely elevated in smokers.
pyqNote: PLAP being elevated in smokers is a specific NEET PG high-yield fact. Combined with LDH, these are the key markers for seminoma.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 45-year-old woman with MEN2A syndrome is found to have a thyroid nodule. Serum calcitonin is markedly elevated. What is the diagnosis and what is the embryological origin of the tumor cell?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma arising from follicular epithelium
Medullary thyroid carcinoma arising from parafollicular C-cells (neural crest origin)
Follicular thyroid carcinoma arising from thyroglobulin-producing cells
Anaplastic carcinoma arising from dedifferentiated follicular cells
explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arises from parafollicular C-cells of neural crest origin. C-cells secrete CALCITONIN, which is the tumor marker. MTC is associated with RET proto-oncogene mutations and occurs sporadically (70%) or as part of MEN2A (pheochromocytoma + hyperparathyroidism + MTC) or MEN2B (pheochromocytoma + mucosal neuromas + MTC). Calcitonin is used for diagnosis, monitoring, and prognosis. Thyroglobulin is the marker for differentiated thyroid carcinoma (papillary/follicular).
highYield: MTC = C-cells + calcitonin marker + RET mutation. MEN2A: pheochromocytoma + hyperparathyroidism + MTC. Thyroglobulin = differentiated thyroid cancer marker.
pyqNote: MEN2A associations and calcitonin as MTC marker are standard NEET PG questions. RET proto-oncogene is the genetic link.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 35-year-old patient presents with episodic hypertension, palpitations, sweating, and headache. 24-hour urine shows markedly elevated VMA (vanillylmandelic acid). What is the diagnosis?
explanation: VMA (Vanillylmandelic acid) and HMA (Homovanillic acid / HMMA) are urinary metabolites of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine). Markedly elevated 24-hour urine VMA is the classic marker for PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA (adrenal medullary tumor of chromaffin cells). The clinical triad is episodic hypertension, sweating, and palpitations. Neuroblastoma (in children) also elevates urinary catecholamines, but the classic adult scenario is pheochromocytoma. Carcinoid uses 5-HIAA.
highYield: VMA = pheochromocytoma (adult). 24-hour urine collection is the gold standard. HVA/VMA ratio: in neuroblastoma, HVA > VMA. Carcinoid uses 5-HIAA.
pyqNote: VMA in 24-hour urine for pheochromocytoma is repeatedly tested in NEET PG. The episodic hypertension triad is classic.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 50-year-old male with flushing, diarrhea, and right heart valve disease is found to have elevated urinary 5-HIAA. CT scan shows a liver mass. Why does carcinoid syndrome manifest ONLY with hepatic metastases in gastrointestinal primaries?
options: GI carcinoids do not produce serotonin
Serotonin from GI primaries is inactivated by the liver via first-pass effect; hepatic mets release serotonin directly into systemic circulation
Liver metastases produce more aggressive histamine
5-HIAA is produced only by hepatic carcinoid cells
explanation: Carcinoid tumors arise most commonly from midgut (ileum, appendix). They secrete serotonin. In GI primaries WITHOUT hepatic metastases, serotonin enters the portal circulation and is INACTIVATED by the liver (first-pass effect) — so NO carcinoid syndrome. With HEPATIC METASTASES, serotonin bypasses hepatic degradation and enters systemic circulation causing carcinoid syndrome: diarrhea, flushing, bronchospasm, and right-sided heart valve disease (tricuspid regurgitation/pulmonary stenosis — left side protected by lungs metabolizing serotonin). 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is the urinary serotonin metabolite used as the marker.
highYield: 5-HIAA = carcinoid marker. Carcinoid syndrome requires hepatic mets (GI primary) or direct systemic secretion (lung/ovarian carcinoid). Right heart valves affected because lungs degrade serotonin before it reaches left heart.
pyqNote: The mechanism of why carcinoid syndrome requires hepatic mets is a classic NEET PG question. 5-HIAA in urine = carcinoid diagnosis.

subject: Pathology
stem: A child is diagnosed with small cell lung cancer-like histology, elevated NSE, and urinary catecholamines. Which tumor is MOST likely?
explanation: NSE (Neuron-Specific Enolase) is elevated in: (1) Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) — the primary marker in adults, (2) Neuroblastoma in children — along with elevated urinary catecholamines (VMA, HVA). NSE is a glycolytic enzyme from neurons and neuroendocrine cells. Neuroblastoma is the most common extracranial solid tumor in children, arising from adrenal medulla/sympathetic ganglia. Wilms tumor marker is none specific; hepatoblastoma uses AFP.
highYield: NSE markers: SCLC in adults, neuroblastoma in children. Neuroblastoma also has elevated VMA/HVA. Hepatoblastoma = AFP. Wilms = no specific marker.
pyqNote: NSE for neuroblastoma vs small cell lung cancer is a NEET PG differentiator. Child + elevated NSE + catecholamines = neuroblastoma.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 45-year-old woman with breast cancer undergoes HER2/neu testing. FISH shows HER2 gene amplification (ratio >2.2). What is the clinical implication?
options: HER2 amplification indicates better prognosis and no need for targeted therapy
HER2 amplification (chr 17q12) indicates aggressive disease and eligibility for trastuzumab (Herceptin) therapy
HER2 amplification is associated with BRCA1 mutation and PARP inhibitor use
HER2 amplification is only relevant in gastric cancer, not breast cancer
explanation: HER2/neu (c-erbB2) is a receptor tyrosine kinase encoded on chromosome 17q12. Amplification occurs in 20-30% of breast cancers and is associated with aggressive behavior and worse prognosis. HER2-positive tumors are eligible for targeted therapy with TRASTUZUMAB (Herceptin), a monoclonal antibody against HER2 extracellular domain. HER2 is also amplified in gastric and gastroesophageal junction cancers. Testing is by IHC (3+ = positive) or FISH (ratio HER2:CEP17 >2.2 = amplified).
highYield: HER2/neu = breast + gastric cancer; amplified gene on chr 17; trastuzumab target; tested by FISH/IHC. Poor prognosis when amplified.
pyqNote: HER2/neu amplification with trastuzumab therapy is a high-yield NEET PG pharmacology-pathology combined question.

subject: Pathology
stem: The Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) creates the BCR-ABL fusion gene. What is the molecular mechanism by which this drives leukemogenesis?
options: BCR-ABL creates a nuclear transcription factor that activates MYC
BCR-ABL creates a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives uncontrolled cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis
BCR-ABL methylates tumor suppressor genes causing epigenetic silencing
BCR-ABL increases RAS GTPase activity causing cell cycle arrest
explanation: The Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)(q34;q11) results in the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene. The resultant BCR-ABL protein is a CONSTITUTIVELY ACTIVE TYROSINE KINASE (constantly signaling without ligand activation). This drives: uncontrolled proliferation, inhibition of apoptosis, and abnormal cell adhesion. This is the molecular basis of CML (and some ALL). Imatinib (Gleevec) is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that competitively inhibits the ATP-binding site of BCR-ABL — a landmark targeted therapy.
highYield: BCR-ABL = constitutively active tyrosine kinase = CML. t(9;22) = Philadelphia chromosome. Imatinib targets BCR-ABL. First successful molecular targeted cancer therapy.
pyqNote: Philadelphia chromosome mechanism and imatinib therapy for CML is a perennial NEET PG/USMLE question. The 'constitutively active' kinase concept is key.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 25-year-old African child presents with a jaw mass. Histology shows a 'starry sky' pattern. Cytogenetics shows t(8;14). What oncogene is activated and what is the mechanism?
options: BCL-2 overexpression due to t(8;14) causing anti-apoptosis
MYC translocation to IgH locus causing constitutive MYC overexpression and uncontrolled cell proliferation
RAS point mutation causing downstream MAPK activation
RET rearrangement causing receptor tyrosine kinase activation
explanation: Burkitt lymphoma is characterized by t(8;14)(q24;q32), which juxtaposes the MYC proto-oncogene (chromosome 8) with the IgH (immunoglobulin heavy chain) locus (chromosome 14). This causes constitutive overexpression of c-MYC, a transcription factor that drives cell cycle progression (G1→S). The 'starry sky' pattern = tingible body macrophages (stars) in a sea of lymphoma cells (sky). EBV association in endemic (African) Burkitt. Less common variants: t(2;8) — kappa light chain; t(8;22) — lambda light chain.
highYield: Burkitt lymphoma: t(8;14) → MYC overexpression. Starry sky histology. EBV association. Jaw mass in endemic form. Fastest growing human tumor (doubling time ~24 hours).
pyqNote: t(8;14) and MYC in Burkitt lymphoma with starry sky pattern is a classic NEET PG/AIIMS question. Know all three Burkitt translocations.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 3-year-old child presents with leukocoria (white reflex in the eye). His mother had bilateral retinoblastoma as a child. What is the 'two-hit hypothesis' in this context?
options: Two separate DNA strands must be methylated to silence RB1
First hit = germline RB1 mutation (inherited); second hit = somatic mutation of remaining allele in retinal cell causes loss of heterozygosity and tumor development
Two RAS mutations required for transformation
Two copies of the RB1 gene must be amplified for retinoblastoma
explanation: Knudson's 'Two-Hit Hypothesis': RB1 tumor suppressor on chromosome 13q14 follows the two-hit model. Hereditary (familial) retinoblastoma: First hit = germline mutation (all cells have one defective RB1 allele); Second hit = somatic mutation of the remaining normal allele in a retinal cell → loss of heterozygosity → pRb loss → uncontrolled cell division. Sporadic: both hits are somatic (later onset, unilateral). RB1 mutation also predisposes to osteosarcoma. pRb normally inhibits E2F transcription factors preventing G1→S transition.
highYield: Two-hit hypothesis = Knudson. RB1: chr 13q14. Hereditary = bilateral, earlier onset. Sporadic = unilateral, later. RB1 loss also causes osteosarcoma.
pyqNote: Two-hit hypothesis for RB1 is one of the most tested tumor suppressor concepts in NEET PG. Know that pRb blocks E2F (G1→S checkpoint).

subject: Pathology
stem: TP53 is mutated in >50% of all human cancers. What is the NORMAL function of p53 protein?
options: p53 is a receptor tyrosine kinase that activates growth signals
p53 is the 'guardian of the genome' — activated by DNA damage, it causes cell cycle arrest (via p21) and apoptosis (via BAX), preventing replication of damaged DNA
p53 methylates histone proteins to activate tumor suppressor genes
p53 directly degrades oncoproteins via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway
explanation: p53 (encoded by TP53, chromosome 17p13) is called the 'GUARDIAN OF THE GENOME.' When DNA is damaged: p53 is stabilized (normally degraded by MDM2) → activates p21 (CDK inhibitor) → G1/S cell cycle arrest → allows DNA repair → if irreparable, p53 activates BAX (pro-apoptotic) → apoptosis. TP53 loss-of-function = replication of damaged DNA → accumulation of mutations → cancer. Li-Fraumeni syndrome = germline TP53 mutation (multiple cancers in family). Mutations in TP53 are the most common genetic alterations in human cancer.
highYield: p53 = guardian of the genome. Activated by DNA damage. p21 mediates G1 arrest. BAX mediates apoptosis. Li-Fraumeni = germline TP53 mutation. Most mutated gene in human cancer.
pyqNote: p53 function and Li-Fraumeni syndrome are tested repeatedly in NEET PG. The p53 → p21 (arrest) / BAX (apoptosis) pathway is essential.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 35-year-old woman is found to have a BRCA1 germline mutation. Which cancers is she at HIGHEST risk for?
options: Breast cancer and ovarian cancer (hereditary breast-ovarian cancer syndrome)
explanation: BRCA1 (chromosome 17q21) and BRCA2 (chromosome 13q12) are DNA repair genes involved in homologous recombination. Germline mutations cause Hereditary Breast-Ovarian Cancer (HBOC) syndrome. BRCA1 carriers: 70-85% lifetime risk of breast cancer; 40-60% risk of ovarian cancer. BRCA2 carriers: also risk of male breast cancer, pancreatic cancer, and prostate cancer. BRCA1/2 mutations are autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance. PARP inhibitors (olaparib) exploit synthetic lethality in BRCA-mutated tumors.
highYield: BRCA1 (17q21) = breast + ovarian cancer. BRCA2 (13q12) = breast + ovarian + male breast + pancreatic. PARP inhibitors for BRCA-mutated cancers.
pyqNote: BRCA1/BRCA2 chromosome locations and associated cancers are NEET PG high-yield. PARP inhibitor mechanism (synthetic lethality) is a newer addition.

subject: Pathology
stem: A patient with >100 colonic polyps and a germline APC mutation develops colorectal cancer at age 30. What syndrome is this and what does APC normally do?
options: Lynch syndrome — APC normally repairs mismatch DNA
Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) — APC normally degrades beta-catenin, suppressing WNT pathway activation and cell proliferation
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome — APC regulates mTOR signaling
HNPCC — APC is a mismatch repair gene
explanation: APC (Adenomatous Polyposis Coli) gene on chromosome 5q21 is mutated in FAP (Familial Adenomatous Polyposis). APC normally promotes degradation of BETA-CATENIN via the destruction complex (APC/AXIN/GSK3B). Without functional APC, beta-catenin accumulates → enters nucleus → activates WNT target genes (MYC, cyclin D1) → cell proliferation. Loss of APC is the FIRST step in the Vogelstein colorectal cancer sequence (APC → KRAS → SMAD4 → TP53). FAP: >100 polyps, virtually 100% risk of colorectal cancer without colectomy.
highYield: APC gene: FAP syndrome. APC degrades beta-catenin (WNT pathway). First hit in colorectal carcinogenesis (Vogelstein sequence). >100 polyps = FAP.
pyqNote: APC and the Vogelstein sequence for colorectal carcinogenesis is a classic NEET PG question. APC → KRAS → SMAD4/DCC → TP53 mutations in sequence.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 40-year-old male with a history of renal cell carcinoma (clear cell type) is found to have cerebellar hemangioblastomas and retinal angiomas. Genetic testing shows a VHL gene mutation. What does VHL normally do?
options: VHL is a GTPase-activating protein that inactivates RAS
VHL targets HIF-1alpha for ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation; loss of VHL causes HIF-1alpha accumulation → VEGF and EPO overexpression → tumor angiogenesis
VHL phosphorylates Rb protein to allow G1/S transition
VHL activates p53 in response to hypoxia
explanation: VHL (Von Hippel-Lindau) tumor suppressor on chromosome 3p25 is part of an E3 ubiquitin ligase complex that ubiquitinates HIF-1alpha (Hypoxia-Inducible Factor-1alpha) for proteasomal degradation under normoxic conditions. When VHL is mutated/lost: HIF-1alpha accumulates → transcribes VEGF (angiogenesis), EPO (erythropoiesis), PDGF, TGF-alpha. VHL syndrome: bilateral clear cell RCC + cerebellar/retinal hemangioblastomas + pheochromocytoma + pancreatic cysts. This is why clear cell RCC is highly vascular.
highYield: VHL → degrades HIF-1alpha. Loss of VHL → HIF-1alpha → VEGF overexpression → angiogenesis → clear cell RCC. VHL syndrome includes hemangioblastomas and pheochromocytoma.
pyqNote: VHL and HIF-1alpha pathway is a high-yield NEET PG question connecting molecular pathology to the clinical features of VHL syndrome.

subject: Pathology
stem: RAS oncogene is mutated in many human cancers. What is the NORMAL function of RAS and how does a point mutation in RAS lead to oncogenesis?
options: Normal RAS is a constitutively active GTPase; mutation inactivates it causing cell cycle arrest

Normal RAS is a GTPase that hydrolyzes GTP→GDP to INACTIVATE signaling; point mutation (codon 12/13/61) impairs GTPase activity → RAS locked in GTP-bound active state → continuous downstream MAPK/PI3K signaling
RAS is a nuclear transcription factor normally repressing MYC
RAS normally activates TP53 to initiate apoptosis
explanation: RAS is a membrane-associated G-protein. Normal function: GTP-bound RAS is ACTIVE (signals to MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways) → RAS intrinsic GTPase hydrolyzes GTP to GDP → RAS becomes INACTIVE. Point mutations at codons 12, 13, or 61 impair GTPase activity → RAS permanently locked in GTP-bound (active) conformation → constitutive downstream signaling → uncontrolled proliferation. KRAS is the most commonly mutated RAS isoform (pancreatic cancer 90%, colorectal 40%, lung adenocarcinoma 30%).
highYield: RAS mutations (codon 12/13/61) impair GTPase activity → locked in active state → constitutive MAPK signaling. KRAS most common isoform mutated. KRAS mutation in pancreatic cancer (90%).
pyqNote: RAS mechanism (GTPase lock) is a fundamental NEET PG oncogene concept. KRAS mutations in colorectal/pancreatic/lung cancers are high-yield.

subject: Pathology
stem: AFP is physiologically elevated in which of the following conditions, and what is the significance of elevated AFP in an adult without liver disease or germ cell tumor?
options: Elevated AFP in an adult without liver disease is always benign

AFP is normally produced by fetal liver and yolk sac; in adults, significantly elevated AFP raises suspicion for HCC or germ cell tumor; AFP is also elevated in ataxia-telangiectasia and during pregnancy (maternal serum AFP screens for NTDs)
AFP is elevated in cirrhosis regardless of HCC development
Adult AFP elevation occurs exclusively in hepatitis B carriers
explanation: AFP is physiologically produced by: (1) fetal liver (major site), (2) yolk sac. After birth, levels fall to <10 ng/mL in adults. Pathological elevations in adults: HCC, yolk sac tumor, and notably ATAXIA-TELANGIECTASIA (hereditary disorder with cerebellar ataxia, telangiectasias, and immunodeficiency — persistently elevated AFP is characteristic). Maternal serum AFP screening: elevated in NTDs (open neural tube defects), decreased in Down syndrome. NOT elevated in choriocarcinoma.
highYield: AFP elevated in: fetal life (normal), HCC, yolk sac tumor, ataxia-telangiectasia. NOT in choriocarcinoma. Maternal AFP elevated in NTDs (spina bifida).
pyqNote: Ataxia-telangiectasia + elevated AFP is a NEET PG trap question. Combined with the NTD screening context, AFP questions appear frequently.

subject: Pathology
stem: A patient with MEN2B has mucosal neuromas, marfanoid habitus, and a thyroid mass. Genetic testing shows a germline RET mutation. Which type of thyroid cancer is associated with RET rearrangements in sporadic cases?
options: Follicular thyroid carcinoma — RET rearrangements drive hematogenous spread
Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma — RET activates dedifferentiation
Papillary thyroid carcinoma — RET/PTC rearrangements are characteristic somatic alterations; germline RET point mutations cause MEN2/MTC
Medullary thyroid carcinoma — RET rearrangements (not point mutations) cause MEN2B
explanation: RET proto-oncogene involvement in thyroid cancer occurs via TWO distinct mechanisms: (1) RET/PTC rearrangements (chromosomal inversions/translocations) are found in sporadic PAPILLARY THYROID CARCINOMA (especially post-radiation exposure); (2) Germline POINT MUTATIONS in RET cause MEN2A (C634R) and MEN2B (M918T) — predisposing to MEDULLARY THYROID CARCINOMA. MEN2B has the most aggressive MTC, marfanoid habitus, mucosal neuromas. RET germline screening allows prophylactic thyroidectomy in mutation carriers.
highYield: RET: two roles — somatic rearrangements (RET/PTC) in papillary thyroid CA; germline point mutations in MEN2/medullary thyroid CA. MEN2B = M918T mutation, most aggressive.
pyqNote: RET dual role (PTC rearrangement vs MEN2 point mutation) is a nuanced NEET PG question. Know MEN2A vs MEN2B RET mutations.

subject: Pathology
stem: NF1 (neurofibromatosis type 1) gene encodes neurofibromin. How does neurofibromin function as a tumor suppressor, and what cancers are associated with NF1 loss?
options: Neurofibromin inhibits PI3K directly; NF1 loss causes breast cancer
Neurofibromin is a RAS-GAP (GTPase-activating protein) that inactivates RAS; NF1 loss → RAS remains active → MAPK signaling → neurofibromas, MPNSTs, optic gliomas, leukemia (JMML)
Neurofibromin phosphorylates pRb to maintain G1 arrest; NF1 loss causes osteosarcoma
Neurofibromin is a transcription factor suppressing MYC; NF1 loss causes lymphoma
explanation: Neurofibromin (NF1 gene product) is a RAS-GAP (GTPase-Activating Protein) that stimulates the intrinsic GTPase activity of RAS → inactivates RAS by accelerating GTP hydrolysis. NF1 LOSS → RAS stays active → uncontrolled MAPK signaling → neurofibromas (plexiform), café-au-lait spots, Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas). Associated malignancies: Malignant Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumors (MPNSTs), optic pathway gliomas, JMML (juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia). NF2 encodes merlin (schwannomin), a cytoskeletal protein; NF2 loss causes bilateral vestibular schwannomas.
highYield: NF1 = neurofibromin = RAS-GAP. NF1 loss → RAS activation. Café-au-lait + neurofibromas + Lisch nodules + MPNST risk. NF2 = merlin = bilateral schwannomas.
pyqNote: NF1 mechanism (RAS-GAP) connecting it to RAS signaling is high-yield for NEET PG. NF1 vs NF2 clinical features distinction is also tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 65-year-old male with known follicular thyroid carcinoma undergoes total thyroidectomy. Which tumor marker is used to monitor for recurrence?
explanation: THYROGLOBULIN is the tumor marker used to monitor recurrence of DIFFERENTIATED thyroid carcinomas (papillary and follicular). After total thyroidectomy, thyroglobulin levels should fall to undetectable. Rising thyroglobulin indicates recurrent or metastatic disease. It is also elevated during thyroid cancer treatment follow-up after radioiodine ablation. CALCITONIN is the marker for medullary thyroid carcinoma (C-cells). CEA is also elevated in medullary thyroid carcinoma (useful as secondary marker).
highYield: Thyroglobulin = differentiated thyroid cancer (papillary/follicular) — post-thyroidectomy monitoring. Calcitonin = medullary thyroid carcinoma (C-cells). Both are distinct.
pyqNote: Thyroglobulin for differentiated thyroid cancer vs calcitonin for medullary carcinoma is a basic but frequently tested NEET PG distinction.

subject: Pathology
stem: Beta-hCG is elevated in a young male with a testicular tumor. Histological examination shows biphasic pattern of cytotrophoblasts and syncytiotrophoblasts WITHOUT chorionic villi. There is also gynecomastia. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: Choriocarcinoma of the testis is a non-seminomatous germ cell tumor characterized by: (1) MARKEDLY elevated beta-hCG (highest of all testicular tumors), (2) Biphasic pattern: cytotrophoblasts + syncytiotrophoblasts, (3) NO chorionic villi (distinguishes from hydatidiform mole), (4) GYNECOMASTIA — syncytiotrophoblasts produce hCG which has LH-like activity → stimulates Leydig cells to produce estrogen → gynecomastia. Choriocarcinoma is highly aggressive with early hematogenous spread (lungs first). AFP is NOT elevated.
highYield: Choriocarcinoma: highest beta-hCG, no chorionic villi, gynecomastia (hCG → estrogen via Leydig cells). AFP NOT elevated. Lung mets first.
pyqNote: Gynecomastia mechanism in choriocarcinoma (hCG → Leydig cells → estrogen) is a classic NEET PG explanation question.

subject: Pathology
stem: Ewing sarcoma in a 15-year-old male shows elevated LDH and NSE. Cytogenetics reveals t(11;22). What gene fusion results, and why are LDH and NSE elevated?
options: BCR-ABL fusion; LDH elevated due to CML involvement of bone marrow
EWSR1-FLI1 fusion gene; LDH indicates rapid cell turnover (poor prognosis); NSE elevated because Ewing sarcoma has neuroectodermal differentiation
COL1A1-PDGFB fusion; LDH elevated from muscle breakdown
PAX3-FOXO1 fusion; NSE elevated due to skeletal muscle origin
explanation: Ewing sarcoma is characterized by t(11;22)(q24;q12) creating the EWSR1-FLI1 fusion gene (EWS-FLI1), a chimeric transcription factor driving oncogenesis. LDH is elevated in Ewing sarcoma and reflects high tumor cell turnover (high LDH = poor prognosis marker). NSE elevation reflects neuroectodermal differentiation (Ewing sarcoma belongs to the Ewing family of tumors/primitive neuroectodermal tumors — PNETs). Classic presentation: diaphyseal bone lesion with 'onion-skin' periosteal reaction in a teenage male.
highYield: Ewing sarcoma: t(11;22) → EWSR1-FLI1 fusion. LDH + NSE elevated. Onion-skin periosteal reaction. Neuroectodermal differentiation. Age 5-20 years.
pyqNote: Ewing sarcoma cytogenetics t(11;22) and the EWSR1-FLI1 fusion are tested in NEET PG. NSE elevation from neuroectodermal differentiation is a key fact.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 55-year-old male smoker has an elevated CEA level of 12 ng/mL (normal <2.5). He has no known malignancy. Which statement about CEA elevation in smokers is CORRECT?
options: CEA elevation in smokers is always due to occult lung cancer and requires immediate bronchoscopy
CEA is falsely elevated in smokers due to colonic mucosal irritation and is not diagnostic of cancer; it must be interpreted in clinical context
Smoking lowers CEA by competing with tumor-produced CEA for binding sites
CEA is not elevated in smokers under any circumstances
explanation: CEA is a glycoprotein that can be non-specifically elevated in SMOKERS (tobacco carcinogens stimulate CEA production from colonic mucosa), as well as in liver cirrhosis, IBD, pancreatitis, and benign breast disease. Elevated CEA in a smoker without known malignancy does NOT diagnose cancer. CEA lacks specificity for diagnosis and is best used for MONITORING RECURRENCE in patients with known colorectal cancer after resection. A baseline CEA before surgery followed by serial monitoring post-operatively is the gold standard use.
highYield: CEA falsely elevated in: smokers, cirrhosis, IBD, pancreatitis. CEA = monitoring recurrence tool, NOT diagnosis or screening. Elevated in many GI cancers.
pyqNote: Smoking causing CEA elevation (false positive) is a NEET PG trap. Always remember CEA is neither sensitive nor specific enough for diagnosis.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 30-year-old woman with known ovarian serous carcinoma has CA-125 of 890 U/mL before chemotherapy. After 3 cycles, CA-125 falls to 45 U/mL. What does this indicate?
options: The patient is cured as CA-125 is within normal range now
CA-125 decline indicates response to chemotherapy; however, CA-125 normalization does not confirm complete pathological remission; serial monitoring continues
CA-125 fall indicates drug toxicity killing normal cells
The CA-125 measurement is not useful once chemotherapy has started
explanation: CA-125 is the PRIMARY MONITORING MARKER for ovarian serous carcinoma (type II, high-grade). A significant fall in CA-125 (>50% from baseline after 1-2 cycles, or normalization) indicates RESPONSE TO CHEMOTHERAPY. However, CA-125 normalization does NOT guarantee complete pathological response — microscopic disease may persist. Rising CA-125 during/after treatment indicates progression/relapse. CA-125 is not a cure marker — it is used for treatment monitoring and early detection of relapse (often precedes clinical relapse by months).
highYield: CA-125 falling with chemotherapy = treatment response. Normalization ≠ cure. Rising CA-125 after complete response = relapse. Used serially for monitoring.
pyqNote: CA-125 interpretation in ovarian cancer treatment response vs cure is a clinical reasoning question in NEET PG and DNB.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 45-year-old man presents with fatigue, fever, and bleeding gums. Peripheral blood smear shows blast cells with needle-like cytoplasmic inclusions that stain positive with myeloperoxidase. These inclusions are formed by fusion of which organelle?
explanation: Auer rods are pathognomonic of AML. They are formed by the abnormal fusion of primary (azurophilic) granules and therefore stain strongly positive for myeloperoxidase (MPO), Sudan black B, and chloroacetate esterase. They are never seen in ALL, making them diagnostically critical. They appear as pink or red needle-like cytoplasmic inclusions in blast cells.
highYield: Auer rods = fused primary granules = MPO positive = pathognomonic for AML (not ALL). Finding Auer rods in a blast cell rules in AML and rules out ALL.
pyqNote: NEET PG frequently asks about the composition and staining characteristics of Auer rods and their diagnostic significance in distinguishing AML from ALL.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with severe bleeding, ecchymoses, and gum bleeding. CBC shows WBC 3,000/μL with 80% blast cells containing numerous Auer rods arranged in bundles ('faggot cells'). PT and aPTT are both prolonged. Fibrinogen is 80 mg/dL (low). Which translocation is most likely responsible for this presentation?
explanation: This is Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL / AML M3) caused by t(15;17), which fuses the PML gene on chromosome 15 with the RARA gene on chromosome 17, producing the PML-RARA fusion protein. This blocks differentiation at the promyelocytic stage. Faggot cells (multiple Auer rods bundled together) are characteristic. The DIC (low fibrinogen, elevated PT/aPTT) occurs due to release of procoagulant granule contents. Treatment is ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) + arsenic trioxide, which induces differentiation of the malignant promyelocytes.
highYield: APL (M3): t(15;17) → PML-RARA → faggot cells → DIC → ATRA+ATO treatment. ATRA works by releasing the differentiation block. DIC is the feared early complication and cause of death.
pyqNote: APL is the highest-yield single AML subtype in NEET PG. The translocation, faggot cells, DIC complication, and ATRA treatment are all frequently tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 55-year-old man is diagnosed with AML. Cytogenetic analysis shows t(8;21)(q22;q22). Which of the following statements about his prognosis and FAB subtype is MOST accurate?
options: FAB M2 with good prognosis; associated with RUNX1-RUNX1T1 fusion
FAB M3 with good prognosis; associated with PML-RARA fusion
FAB M4 with intermediate prognosis; associated with CBFbeta-MYH11 fusion
FAB M1 with poor prognosis; associated with complex cytogenetics
explanation: t(8;21)(q22;q22) is the most common AML translocation and is classified as FAB M2 (AML with maturation). It creates the RUNX1-RUNX1T1 (formerly AML1-ETO) fusion protein. This translocation confers GOOD prognosis and is a core-binding factor (CBF) AML. Other favorable cytogenetics include inv(16) seen in M4Eo. Poor prognosis is associated with t(6;9), complex cytogenetics (≥3 abnormalities), and monosomal karyotype. t(15;17) is M3 (APL). inv(16) → CBFbeta-MYH11 fusion is M4Eo.
highYield: Favorable AML cytogenetics: t(8;21) → M2 RUNX1-RUNX1T1; inv(16) → M4Eo CBFbeta-MYH11. Poor prognosis: t(6;9), complex karyotype, FLT3-ITD mutation, monosomy 5/7.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests AML prognostic groups via cytogenetics. Know that t(8;21) = good, t(6;9) = poor, inv(16) = good.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with gum hypertrophy and swelling, skin infiltration (leukemia cutis), and lymphadenopathy. Peripheral smear shows blasts with folded/kidney-shaped nuclei. Nonspecific esterase (NSE) stain is positive; MPO is weakly positive. Which AML subtype is most consistent with this presentation?
explanation: AML M4 (myelomonocytic) and M5 (monocytic) show prominent extramedullary infiltration, particularly gum hypertrophy (gingival hyperplasia), skin infiltration (leukemia cutis), and CNS involvement. Monocytic blasts have folded, kidney-shaped, or horseshoe-shaped nuclei. Nonspecific esterase (NSE/alpha-naphthyl acetate esterase) is the key positive stain, differentiating monocytic from granulocytic blasts. MPO may be weakly positive. Auer rods can be seen in monocytes in M4/M5. The classic question trigger is 'gum infiltration' → think monocytic AML.
highYield: Gum hypertrophy + leukemia cutis + NSE positive + kidney-shaped nuclei = monocytic AML (M4/M5). NSE is the stain; MPO weakly positive or negative.
pyqNote: Gingival infiltration is a classic NEET PG trigger for AML M4/M5. The NSE stain and the morphological features of monocytic blasts are frequently asked.

subject: Pathology
stem: Flow cytometry is performed on blast cells from a patient with newly diagnosed AML. Which combination of surface markers is MOST characteristic of myeloid blasts?
explanation: Myeloid blasts in AML characteristically express CD13 (myeloid), CD33 (pan-myeloid), CD117 (c-KIT, stem cell marker), and cytoplasmic MPO (myeloperoxidase). CD34 and HLA-DR are also commonly expressed. CD3/CD4/CD8 are T-cell markers. CD19/CD20/CD22/CD79a are B-cell markers. CD5+CD23+CD19 is the classic CLL phenotype. In APL (M3), HLA-DR is notably absent, which helps distinguish it from other AML subtypes on flow cytometry.
highYield: AML immunophenotype: CD13, CD33, CD117 (c-KIT), MPO. APL (M3) is HLA-DR negative — an important diagnostic clue. CD34 negativity in APL is also characteristic.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests flow cytometry markers to distinguish AML from ALL and to identify AML subtypes. The APL HLA-DR negative pattern is a favourite.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 50-year-old man presents with massive splenomegaly, weight loss, and night sweats. WBC is 1,20,000/μL with a full spectrum of myeloid cells at all stages of maturation. Leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score is markedly LOW. Which molecular abnormality is pathognomonic for this condition?
options: BCR-ABL1 fusion gene on Philadelphia chromosome
explanation: This presentation is classic for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). The Philadelphia chromosome (Ph) results from a reciprocal translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11), creating the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene on the shortened chromosome 22. The BCR-ABL1 protein is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives uncontrolled myeloid proliferation. The LAP score is characteristically LOW in CML (differentiating from leukemoid reaction where LAP is HIGH). Basophilia is another characteristic finding. JAK2 V617F is associated with polycythemia vera and other MPNs.
highYield: CML: t(9;22) → BCR-ABL1 (constitutively active tyrosine kinase) → Ph chromosome on ch22 (shortened). LAP score LOW in CML, HIGH in leukemoid reaction — a classic NEET PG differentiator.
pyqNote: Philadelphia chromosome and LAP score are among the most frequently tested CML facts in NEET PG. The t(9;22) translocation mechanism is also regularly asked.

subject: Pathology
stem: A patient known to have CML on imatinib therapy for 3 years presents with worsening splenomegaly, fever, and bone pain. CBC shows WBC 1,80,000/μL with 22% blasts, 10% basophils, and thrombocytopenia. Which phase of CML is this patient most likely in?
explanation: CML progresses through three phases: (1) Chronic phase: <10% blasts, responds to therapy; (2) Accelerated phase: 10-19% blasts OR basophils ≥20% OR new cytogenetic abnormalities OR refractory thrombocytopenia/thrombocytosis; (3) Blast crisis: ≥20% blasts in blood or marrow (or extramedullary blast infiltrates). This patient has 22% blasts crossing the threshold into blast crisis, but the question can be debated — however 22% blasts meets the WHO blast crisis criterion (≥20%). The presence of 10% basophils and thrombocytopenia is typical of accelerated phase features. Blast crisis can be myeloid (70%) or lymphoid (30%, can respond to ALL-type therapy). Allogeneic SCT is the treatment of choice in blast crisis.
highYield: CML phases: Chronic (<10% blasts) → Accelerated (10-19% blasts / basophilia ≥20%) → Blast crisis (≥20% blasts). Allogeneic SCT is indicated in blast crisis.
pyqNote: The blast percentage cutoffs for CML phases are frequently tested in NEET PG. Blast crisis treatment (allo-SCT) is also commonly asked.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 42-year-old man is newly diagnosed with CML in chronic phase. BCR-ABL1 is confirmed by FISH. Which treatment is the most appropriate first-line therapy, and what is its mechanism of action?
options: Imatinib — competitive inhibitor of BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase at ATP binding site
Interferon-alpha — stimulates NK cell cytotoxicity against Ph+ cells
explanation: Imatinib (Gleevec) is the standard first-line treatment for CML in chronic phase. It is a selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) that competitively inhibits the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein at the ATP-binding site, blocking the constitutive kinase activity that drives CML proliferation. Second-generation TKIs include dasatinib (also inhibits SRC kinase) and nilotinib — used in imatinib-resistant or intolerant cases. The T315I 'gatekeeper' mutation confers resistance to imatinib, dasatinib, and nilotinib, but is sensitive to ponatinib (third-generation TKI). Prior to imatinib, allogeneic SCT was the only curative option.
highYield: Imatinib = first-line CML. Mechanism: blocks BCR-ABL1 ATP binding site. T315I mutation = resistance to 1st/2nd gen TKIs → ponatinib. Allogeneic SCT = curative option in blast crisis.
pyqNote: The mechanism of imatinib and TKI resistance mechanisms (T315I mutation) are high-yield NEET PG topics in CML treatment.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 60-year-old woman with known tuberculosis presents with WBC 55,000/μL with a left shift. To differentiate CML from a leukemoid reaction, leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score is performed. Which result is expected in CML?
options: LAP score is not helpful in this differentiation
explanation: Leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score measures the enzyme activity in mature neutrophils. In CML, the malignant neutrophils are functionally abnormal and show markedly LOW LAP scores (<10), reflecting their inability to perform normal enzymatic functions. In leukemoid reactions (reactive leukocytosis due to infection, inflammation, malignancy, etc.), LAP score is markedly HIGH (>100), as the reactive neutrophils are normally functioning. This LAP score difference is a classic and frequently tested discriminator between CML and leukemoid reaction in NEET PG.
highYield: LAP score: CML = LOW (<10); Leukemoid reaction = HIGH (>100). This is a MUST-KNOW differentiator for NEET PG. Polycythemia vera also has high LAP.
pyqNote: LAP score differentiation between CML and leukemoid reaction is one of the most frequently tested facts in NEET PG hematology.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 68-year-old man is incidentally found to have WBC 85,000/μL on routine CBC. Peripheral blood smear shows small, mature-appearing lymphocytes with clumped chromatin and numerous 'smudge cells' (basket cells). He is asymptomatic. Which is the most likely diagnosis and its characteristic immunophenotype?
options: CLL; CD5+, CD23+, CD19+, weak surface Ig
Mantle cell lymphoma; CD5+, CD23-, cyclin D1+
Hairy cell leukemia; CD25+, CD11c+, TRAP+
explanation: This is classic Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) — the most common leukemia in Western adults (>60 years). Characteristic smudge (smear/basket) cells form because the fragile CLL cells are crushed during smear preparation. The immunophenotype is: CD5+ (usually a T-cell marker, but co-expressed in CLL), CD23+, CD19+, CD20 (dim), and weak (dim) surface immunoglobulin — distinguishing CLL from mantle cell lymphoma (CD5+, CD23-, cyclin D1+, strong surface Ig). CLL cells are monoclonal B cells arrested at an intermediate stage of B-cell development.
highYield: CLL: CD5+CD23+CD19+ with dim surface Ig = classic immunophenotype. Smudge cells on smear = pathognomonic appearance. Most common adult leukemia in Western countries.
pyqNote: CLL smudge cells and the CD5+CD23+ immunophenotype are the most frequently tested CLL facts in NEET PG. The distinction from mantle cell lymphoma (CD5+ but CD23-) is also important.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 72-year-old woman with known CLL develops progressive dyspnea and pallor. Hemoglobin drops to 6.5 g/dL. Direct Coombs test (DAT) is positive. Peripheral smear shows spherocytes. Serum immunoglobulins are low. Which complication of CLL best explains the anemia?
options: Iron deficiency from chronic blood loss
explanation: CLL is associated with immune dysregulation and can cause warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) — the most common autoimmune complication. Warm AIHA is mediated by IgG antibodies (reactive at 37°C) that coat RBCs, leading to extravascular hemolysis in the spleen. Positive DAT (direct antiglobulin/Coombs test), spherocytes on smear, and low haptoglobin confirm hemolysis. CLL also causes hypogammaglobulinemia (low serum immunoglobulins), increasing susceptibility to bacterial infections. Cold agglutinin disease (IgM mediated) is less common. Steroids are first-line for warm AIHA in CLL.
highYield: CLL complications: warm AIHA (DAT positive, IgG mediated), hypogammaglobulinemia (bacterial infections), immune thrombocytopenia (ITP), and Richter transformation.
pyqNote: Warm AIHA as a complication of CLL, with positive DAT and spherocytes, is a classic NEET PG scenario. Hypogammaglobulinemia in CLL is also frequently tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 65-year-old man with CLL for 8 years develops rapidly enlarging cervical lymphadenopathy, fever, and weight loss. LDH is markedly elevated. Lymph node biopsy shows large cells with vesicular nuclei and prominent nucleoli. What is this complication called, and what is the most common transformation type?
options: Richter transformation → Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL)
explanation: Richter transformation (RT) refers to the development of an aggressive lymphoma in a patient with pre-existing CLL. The most common transformation (95%) is into Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL), characterized by rapidly enlarging lymph nodes, constitutional B symptoms, markedly elevated LDH, and a worsening clinical course. The biopsy shows large cells with prominent nucleoli (centroblastic or immunoblastic morphology). Rarely, transformation can be into Hodgkin lymphoma (5%). RT carries a poor prognosis with median survival of 5-8 months. It is clonally related to the original CLL in ~80% of cases.
highYield: Richter transformation = CLL → DLBCL (most common) or Hodgkin lymphoma (rare). Triggered by: rapidly enlarging nodes, elevated LDH, B symptoms. Poor prognosis.
pyqNote: Richter transformation and its most common subtype (DLBCL) is a frequently tested CLL complication in NEET PG exam papers.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 70-year-old man with CLL has lymphocytosis only with WBC 55,000/μL, no lymphadenopathy, no organomegaly, and normal hemoglobin and platelets. Using the Rai staging system, which stage is he in and what is the recommended management?
options: Rai Stage III (high risk); start FCR immediately
Rai Stage 0 (low risk); watch and wait
Rai Stage II (intermediate risk); start venetoclax+obinutuzumab
explanation: Rai staging for CLL: Stage 0 = lymphocytosis only (low risk, median survival >10 years); Stage I = lymphocytosis + lymphadenopathy (intermediate risk); Stage II = lymphocytosis + splenomegaly/hepatomegaly (intermediate risk); Stage III = lymphocytosis + anemia (Hb <11 g/dL) — high risk; Stage IV = lymphocytosis + thrombocytopenia (platelets <100,000) — high risk. This patient has Stage 0. CLL is treated with 'watch and wait' for early stage (Rai 0-II without active disease symptoms). Treatment is initiated for symptomatic disease, significant cytopenias, progressive lymphocytosis, or bulky disease. FCR (fludarabine+cyclophosphamide+rituximab) is standard for fit patients; venetoclax+obinutuzumab for unfit/elderly.
highYield: Rai Stage 0 = lymphocytosis only = watch and wait. Treatment initiated for symptomatic disease or cytopenias. FCR for fit; venetoclax+obinutuzumab for unfit patients.
pyqNote: Rai staging system and the concept of 'watch and wait' for early-stage CLL are commonly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 22-year-old man presents with painless cervical lymphadenopathy, fever, night sweats, and weight loss of 12 kg over 3 months. Lymph node biopsy shows large cells with bilobed nuclei, prominent 'owl-eye' nucleoli, and a surrounding inflammatory infiltrate. These cells are positive for CD15 and CD30. What is the origin and name of these characteristic cells?
options: Malignant T lymphocytes; called Sezary cells
Malignant B lymphocytes; called Reed-Sternberg cells
Malignant plasma cells; called Mott cells
Malignant macrophages; called Touton giant cells
explanation: Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells are the pathognomonic malignant cells of classical Hodgkin Lymphoma (HL). They are large cells with bilobed or multilobed nuclei containing prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli (one per nuclear lobe). RS cells are derived from germinal center B lymphocytes but have lost most B-cell markers. They are characteristically CD15+ and CD30+ (also called Ki-1). EBV (Epstein-Barr Virus) is associated with HL, especially the mixed cellularity subtype, and can be demonstrated in RS cells by LMP1 staining. B symptoms (fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, weight loss >10% in 6 months) are present.
highYield: Reed-Sternberg cells: bilobed, owl-eye nucleoli, CD15+, CD30+, EBV association, derived from B cells but B-cell marker negative (CD20-). Pathognomonic for classical Hodgkin lymphoma.
pyqNote: RS cell morphology, immunophenotype (CD15+CD30+), and EBV association are the most tested facts about Hodgkin lymphoma in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: Regarding Hodgkin Lymphoma subtypes, which of the following correctly pairs the subtype with its distinguishing features?
options: Nodular Sclerosing — most common; lacunar RS cell variant; collagen bands; mediastinal involvement common
Lymphocyte Predominant — worst prognosis; RS cells abundant; predominantly older males
Lymphocyte Depleted — best prognosis; few RS cells; abundant lymphocytes
Mixed Cellularity — exclusively in young females; EBV negative; nodular fibrosis
explanation: Nodular Sclerosing Hodgkin Lymphoma (NSHL) is the most common subtype (~70%), occurring predominantly in young adults and women, with frequent mediastinal involvement. Characteristic features: collagen bands dividing the node into nodules, and lacunar RS cell variant (retraction artifact in formalin). Lymphocyte Predominant HL (LPHL) has the BEST prognosis, has LP cells (popcorn cells), and is CD20+CD15-CD30- (unlike classical HL). Lymphocyte Depleted HL has the WORST prognosis, most RS cells, fewest lymphocytes, and is seen in elderly/HIV patients. Mixed Cellularity has highest EBV association.
highYield: HL subtypes prognosis: Lymphocyte Predominant (BEST) > Nodular Sclerosing > Mixed Cellularity > Lymphocyte Depleted (WORST). NSHL most common. LPHL has popcorn cells, CD20+.
pyqNote: HL subtype features, especially NSHL (most common) and the best/worst prognosis subtypes, are classic NEET PG exam questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 24-year-old woman with Hodgkin Lymphoma has involvement of cervical and mediastinal lymph nodes on the same side of the diaphragm, with fever and night sweats. Using Ann Arbor staging, she is classified as:
explanation: Ann Arbor Staging: Stage I = single lymph node region; Stage II = two or more node regions on the SAME side of diaphragm; Stage III = node regions on BOTH sides of diaphragm; Stage IV = disseminated involvement of extralymphatic organs (liver, bone marrow, lung, etc.). Suffix A = absence of B symptoms; suffix B = presence of B symptoms (unexplained fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, and unexplained weight loss >10% body weight in 6 months). This patient has cervical + mediastinal nodes (both above diaphragm = Stage II) with B symptoms (fever and night sweats) → Stage II-B.
highYield: Ann Arbor: I (single region) → II (same side of diaphragm) → III (both sides) → IV (extranodal). A = no B symptoms, B = fever/night sweats/weight loss >10%. Contiguous spread is characteristic of HL.
pyqNote: Ann Arbor staging is one of the most frequently tested topics in HL for NEET PG. The B-symptom definitions are commonly MCQ targets.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 30-year-old woman is diagnosed with Stage IIA Nodular Sclerosing Hodgkin Lymphoma. She has a large mediastinal mass on CXR (widening of mediastinum >1/3 of chest width). What is the standard chemotherapy regimen for Hodgkin Lymphoma?
explanation: ABVD (Doxorubicin/Adriamycin + Bleomycin + Vinblastine + Dacarbazine) is the standard first-line chemotherapy for Hodgkin Lymphoma. It replaced MOPP due to fewer long-term toxicities (MOPP caused sterility and secondary AML). Mediastinal widening (>1/3 of chest diameter) is a common presentation of NSHL and indicates bulky disease. CHOP is used for NHL (especially DLBCL). FCR is used for CLL. A key toxicity: bleomycin causes pulmonary fibrosis (dose-limiting). Radiotherapy is combined with chemotherapy for early-stage or bulky disease.
highYield: ABVD = standard HL chemotherapy. Bleomycin toxicity = pulmonary fibrosis. MOPP abandoned due to sterility + secondary AML. NSHL commonly presents with mediastinal widening on CXR.
pyqNote: ABVD regimen components and bleomycin pulmonary toxicity are consistently tested in NEET PG for Hodgkin lymphoma treatment questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a rapidly enlarging abdominal mass, fever, and LDH of 850 U/L (markedly elevated). CT shows a large retroperitoneal mass with bulky lymphadenopathy. Biopsy shows large lymphoid cells with vesicular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and high mitotic rate. CD20 is strongly positive. What is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?
explanation: DLBCL is the most common Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma overall (~30-40% of all NHL). It is an aggressive B-cell lymphoma characterized by rapidly enlarging lymph nodes or extranodal masses, markedly elevated LDH (marker of high tumor burden), and B symptoms. Histology shows large cells with vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli (centroblastic or immunoblastic pattern). Strong CD20 positivity is characteristic. First-line treatment is R-CHOP (Rituximab + Cyclophosphamide + Doxorubicin [Hydroxydaunorubicin] + Oncovin [Vincristine] + Prednisolone). Rituximab is anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody. International Prognostic Index (IPI) guides prognosis.
highYield: DLBCL = most common NHL overall. R-CHOP = first-line. CD20+ = target for rituximab. High LDH = high tumor burden/poor prognosis. Aggressive but potentially curable.
pyqNote: DLBCL being the most common NHL and R-CHOP as first-line treatment are very frequently tested facts in NEET PG oncology/hematology.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 10-year-old African boy presents with a rapidly enlarging jaw mass. Biopsy shows a sheet of medium-sized lymphoid cells with numerous interspersed macrophages containing phagocytosed nuclear debris, giving a 'starry sky' appearance. Ki-67 proliferation index is nearly 100%. Which translocation is characteristically associated with this tumor?
explanation: Burkitt Lymphoma (BL) is characterized by the translocation t(8;14)(q24;q32), which juxtaposes the MYC oncogene (chromosome 8) with the immunoglobulin heavy chain gene (IgH, chromosome 14), causing constitutive MYC overexpression and uncontrolled cell proliferation. The 'starry sky' pattern results from tingible body macrophages (dark stars on pale background of tumor cells) phagocytosing apoptotic debris — reflecting the extremely high proliferation AND apoptosis rate. Endemic BL: jaw in children, EBV-associated. Sporadic BL: ileocecal, HIV-associated in adults. Ki-67 nearly 100% = fastest growing human tumor.
highYield: Burkitt lymphoma: t(8;14) → MYC activation → starry sky pattern → Ki-67 ~100%. Jaw = endemic (African/EBV); ileocecal = sporadic; HIV association = sporadic adult BL.
pyqNote: Burkitt lymphoma t(8;14) MYC translocation, starry sky appearance, and clinical presentations (endemic vs sporadic) are very high-yield NEET PG topics.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with painless, waxing-and-waning lymphadenopathy for 2 years. Lymph node biopsy shows a nodular architecture with small cleaved cells and a few large cells arranged in follicle-like structures. BCL2 protein is strongly overexpressed. Which translocation and treatment approach is most characteristic of this lymphoma?
options: t(8;14); aggressive chemotherapy is curative in most cases
t(14;18); BCL2 overexpression; watch and wait for asymptomatic low-burden disease
t(11;14); cyclin D1 overexpression; ibrutinib first-line
explanation: Follicular Lymphoma (FL) is the most common indolent (low-grade) NHL. The translocation t(14;18)(q32;q21) juxtaposes BCL2 (chromosome 18) with the IgH gene (chromosome 14), causing BCL2 overexpression. BCL2 is an anti-apoptotic protein, so its overexpression prevents apoptosis of follicular B cells, causing their accumulation. FL is characterized by: painless waxing-waning lymphadenopathy (classic), nodular (follicular) architecture, small cleaved centrocytes + large centroblasts, CD20+/CD10+/BCL2+. Management of low-burden asymptomatic FL = 'watch and wait'. Treatment needed: R-CHOP or R-bendamustine. FL can transform to DLBCL (histological transformation).
highYield: Follicular lymphoma: t(14;18) → BCL2 overexpression → anti-apoptotic → indolent. Watch and wait for asymptomatic. Waxing-waning lymphadenopathy is characteristic. Can transform to DLBCL.
pyqNote: Follicular lymphoma t(14;18) and BCL2, together with the 'watch and wait' strategy for asymptomatic disease, are classic NEET PG examination points.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 62-year-old man presents with lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and bone marrow involvement. Biopsy of the lymph node shows small-to-medium lymphoid cells with irregular nuclear contours growing in a vaguely nodular pattern. Immunohistochemistry shows CD5+, CD20+, CD23-, and strong nuclear cyclin D1 positivity. Which translocation is responsible?
explanation: Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) is caused by t(11;14)(q13;q32), which fuses the CCND1 gene (encoding cyclin D1, chromosome 11) with the IgH gene (chromosome 14), causing cyclin D1 overexpression. Cyclin D1 drives cell cycle progression from G1 to S phase. MCL is an aggressive B-cell lymphoma despite its 'small cell' appearance, with median survival of 3-5 years. Characteristic immunophenotype: CD5+, CD20+, CD23- (differentiating from CLL which is CD23+), cyclin D1+ (nuclear, highly specific). MCL affects older males, often with widespread disease at presentation. SOX11 is a useful marker when cyclin D1 is negative.
highYield: Mantle cell lymphoma: t(11;14) → cyclin D1 overexpression. CD5+CD20+CD23-cyclin D1+ (distinguish from CLL which is CD23+). Aggressive despite small cell morphology. SOX11 is sensitive marker.
pyqNote: Mantle cell lymphoma t(11;14)/cyclin D1 and its distinction from CLL (CD23 negative) are high-yield NEET PG exam topics.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 55-year-old woman is found to have gastric MALT lymphoma (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma) on endoscopic biopsy. She tests positive for Helicobacter pylori. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for localized gastric MALT lymphoma?
explanation: Gastric MALT lymphoma is strongly associated with H. pylori infection, which drives chronic antigenic stimulation of gastric B cells. In early-stage (Stage I/II) H. pylori-positive gastric MALT lymphoma, H. pylori eradication (triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin/metronidazole) is the first-line treatment and achieves complete remission in ~75-80% of cases. This is because the lymphoma is antigen-driven and regresses without the antigenic stimulus. Approximately 95% of gastric MALT lymphomas test positive for H. pylori. MALT lymphomas also occur in thyroid (Hashimoto), salivary gland (Sjogren), lung, and orbit. Rituximab or chemotherapy is reserved for H. pylori-negative or refractory cases.
highYield: Gastric MALT lymphoma → H. pylori association → H. pylori eradication as first-line treatment → regression in 75-80% of localized cases. Antigen-driven lymphoma concept is key.
pyqNote: H. pylori association with gastric MALT lymphoma and H. pylori eradication as first-line treatment is a very high-yield NEET PG question that appears repeatedly.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with severe back pain, fatigue, and recurrent pneumonia. Investigations show: hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL, calcium 11.8 mg/dL (elevated), creatinine 3.2 mg/dL (elevated), extensive lytic bone lesions on X-ray spine, and urine dipstick negative for albumin but strongly positive for protein. What is the most likely diagnosis and the mnemonic for its defining organ damage criteria?
options: Metastatic carcinoma; CAMP (Calcium, Anemia, Metastasis, Pain)
Multiple Myeloma; CRAB (Calcium elevated, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions)
Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia; HAEM (Hyperviscosity, Anemia, Enlarged nodes, Marrow infiltration)
CLL with Richter transformation; SLIM (Stage, LDH, IPI, Marrow)
explanation: Multiple Myeloma (MM) is a clonal plasma cell malignancy presenting with the CRAB criteria: hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia (normocytic normochromic), Bone lesions (punched-out lytic, pathological fractures). The urine protein (strongly positive) without albumin on dipstick indicates Bence-Jones protein (free light chains — kappa or lambda) — dipstick detects albumin only, NOT light chains; sulfosalicylic acid (SSA) test or urine protein electrophoresis detects Bence-Jones proteins. Recurrent infections (pneumonia) occur due to hypogammaglobulinemia (despite excess M-protein, normal immunoglobulins are suppressed).
highYield: MM = CRAB + plasma cells >10% BM + M-protein. Bence-Jones protein = urine free light chains. Dipstick negative but SSA positive = classic Bence-Jones scenario in NEET PG.
pyqNote: CRAB criteria for myeloma and the Bence-Jones protein (dipstick negative, SSA positive) scenario are among the most tested myeloma facts in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 70-year-old woman is being evaluated for multiple myeloma. Peripheral blood smear shows RBCs arranged in stacks like coins. Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) shows a tall, narrow spike in the gamma region. Bone marrow biopsy shows 35% plasma cells with eccentric nuclei and a perinuclear 'hof' (clearing). Which peripheral blood smear finding and which serum protein finding are most characteristic?
explanation: Rouleaux formation (RBCs stacked like coins due to elevated serum proteins coating RBC surfaces, decreasing their negative charge) is characteristic of multiple myeloma. The M-protein (monoclonal paraprotein) appears as a tall, narrow 'spike' in the gamma or beta region on SPEP — this is a monoclonal immunoglobulin (usually IgG or IgA) produced by the malignant plasma cell clone. Plasma cells in MM show: eccentric nuclei, abundant basophilic cytoplasm, perinuclear hof (clearing = Golgi zone), and clock-face/cartwheel chromatin. Smudge cells = CLL. Teardrop cells (dacrocytes) = myelofibrosis. Schistocytes = MAHA.
highYield: MM blood smear: rouleaux formation. SPEP: monoclonal M-spike (narrow tall peak in gamma region). Plasma cell morphology: eccentric nucleus, clock-face chromatin, perinuclear hof.
pyqNote: Rouleaux formation and M-spike on SPEP are the most tested laboratory findings of multiple myeloma in NEET PG, often presented as image-based questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 68-year-old man with multiple myeloma undergoes a skeletal survey. X-rays show multiple small, well-circumscribed 'punched-out' lytic lesions in the skull without a sclerotic rim. Why are these lesions 'punched-out' and lytic rather than blastic (sclerotic)?
options: Myeloma cells directly invade cortical bone and replace trabeculae
Myeloma cells secrete RANKL and DKK-1, activating osteoclasts while suppressing osteoblasts
Myeloma cells produce TNF-alpha that activates osteoblasts, causing sclerotic lesions
Myeloma cells directly mineralize bone matrix causing mixed lytic-sclerotic pattern
explanation: Multiple myeloma bone lesions are predominantly osteolytic. Myeloma plasma cells produce cytokines that: (1) activate osteoclasts (via RANKL upregulation, IL-6, MIP-1alpha) — leading to bone resorption; and (2) suppress osteoblasts (via DKK-1, secreted frizzled-related proteins, which inhibit Wnt signaling) — preventing new bone formation. This uncoupling of osteoclast activation and osteoblast suppression results in pure lytic lesions without sclerosis ('punched-out' appearance). Bone scan is NEGATIVE in myeloma (no osteoblastic activity), unlike metastases where bone scan is positive. PET-CT or low-dose whole-body CT is preferred for myeloma staging.
highYield: Myeloma lytic lesions: RANKL activates osteoclasts + DKK-1 suppresses osteoblasts → no sclerosis. Bone scan NEGATIVE in myeloma (no osteoblastic activity). Use CT/PET-CT instead.
pyqNote: The mechanism of lytic bone lesions in myeloma (RANKL/DKK-1) and the negative bone scan finding are frequently tested in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 60-year-old man is newly diagnosed with multiple myeloma. He is fit for transplant. Which initial therapy followed by consolidation is considered the current standard of care?
options: FCR followed by allogeneic stem cell transplant
VRd (Bortezomib + Lenalidomide + Dexamethasone) followed by autologous stem cell transplant
Imatinib followed by allogeneic SCT in blast crisis
explanation: For transplant-eligible multiple myeloma patients, the current standard of care is: (1) Induction with a triplet regimen — VRd (Bortezomib [proteasome inhibitor] + Lenalidomide [IMiD] + Dexamethasone) or daratumumab-VRd (daratumumab = anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody); (2) followed by high-dose melphalan conditioning and autologous stem cell transplant (ASCT) for consolidation; (3) maintenance with lenalidomide. Bortezomib inhibits the 26S proteasome, blocking NF-kB activation and causing plasma cell apoptosis. Novel agents include daratumumab (anti-CD38), elotuzumab (anti-SLAMF7), carfilzomib, and ixazomib. Allogenic SCT is avoided in myeloma due to high treatment-related mortality.
highYield: Myeloma treatment (transplant-eligible): VRd induction → autologous SCT → lenalidomide maintenance. Bortezomib = proteasome inhibitor. Daratumumab = anti-CD38. Novel triplets are standard.
pyqNote: The role of bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor), lenalidomide (IMiD), and autologous SCT in myeloma management are increasingly tested in advanced NEET PG questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 19-year-old man with a history of infectious mononucleosis develops cervical lymphadenopathy 18 months later. Biopsy confirms Hodgkin Lymphoma. Which statement about Hodgkin Lymphoma spread pattern and EBV association is MOST accurate?
options: HL spreads hematogenously to distant sites first; EBV is associated only with lymphocyte-depleted subtype
HL spreads contiguously from one node group to adjacent node groups; EBV is most closely associated with mixed cellularity subtype
HL spreads non-contiguously; EBV is associated with all subtypes equally
HL spreads via lymphatics in a skip pattern; EBV is associated only with nodular sclerosing subtype
explanation: Hodgkin Lymphoma characteristically spreads in a contiguous fashion from one lymph node group to the immediately adjacent group (e.g., cervical → mediastinal → para-aortic). This orderly, contiguous spread is what makes Ann Arbor staging clinically meaningful and allows radiation therapy to be effective in early-stage disease. EBV (Epstein-Barr Virus) is associated with HL in approximately 50% of cases overall, with the highest frequency in the Mixed Cellularity subtype (~70%), followed by Lymphocyte Depleted. EBV association is also seen in HIV-associated HL. EBV LMP1 (latent membrane protein 1) is detectable in RS cells and activates NF-kB.
highYield: HL spread: contiguous (predictable, orderly). EBV association: highest in Mixed Cellularity subtype. EBV LMP1 activates NF-kB in RS cells. This explains effectiveness of radiation in early HL.
pyqNote: The contiguous spread pattern of HL (vs non-contiguous in NHL) and EBV association with the mixed cellularity subtype are classic NEET PG distinction points.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with pallor, fever, and bone pain. Peripheral smear shows 75% blast cells. Special stains are performed: MPO is NEGATIVE, PAS (periodic acid-Schiff) is POSITIVE with block positivity, and TdT (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase) is POSITIVE. Flow cytometry shows CD10+, CD19+, CD34+. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: AML M1 — myeloid origin, MPO positive
B-ALL (B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia) — lymphoid origin
CML blast crisis — myeloid lineage, Ph chromosome
explanation: This is B-cell ALL (B-Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia/Lymphoma). Key distinguishing features from AML: (1) MPO NEGATIVE (AML is MPO positive); (2) PAS positive with BLOCK (chunky) pattern — reflects glycogen in lymphoblasts; (3) TdT POSITIVE — TdT is a nuclear enzyme expressed in immature lymphoblasts (ALL) but NOT in myeloblasts (AML); (4) CD10 (CALLA antigen — common ALL antigen), CD19, CD34 = B-cell lymphoblast markers. B-ALL is the most common childhood leukemia and cancer. The t(12;21) TEL-AML1 translocation confers best prognosis in childhood ALL. Ph chromosome (BCR-ABL1) in ALL = Ph+ ALL = poor prognosis.
highYield: AML vs ALL: MPO+ = AML (myeloid); TdT+ = ALL (lymphoid); PAS block = ALL. CD10+CD19+ = B-ALL; CD3+CD7+ = T-ALL. TdT is the single best marker to identify ALL over AML.
pyqNote: The AML vs ALL differentiation using MPO, TdT, and PAS stains is one of the most frequently tested comparisons in NEET PG hematological malignancy questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 48-year-old man presents with massive splenomegaly. CBC shows WBC 1,40,000/μL with a full myeloid spectrum on differential count: neutrophils, band forms, metamyelocytes, myelocytes, promyelocytes, and notably increased basophils (12%). Eosinophilia is also present. Blasts are <5%. Platelet count is 700,000/μL. Which finding on blood film is MOST characteristic of CML and helps distinguish it from other myeloproliferative neoplasms?
options: Absolute basophilia with full myeloid spectrum and low blast percentage
explanation: Absolute basophilia is one of the most characteristic features of CML and is present in virtually all cases. Basophils are counted as part of the differential in CML monitoring, and basophilia >20% is one of the criteria for accelerated phase. The full myeloid spectrum (all stages of granulocyte maturation visible in blood, like looking at a bone marrow biopsy in blood — the 'bone marrow in the blood' concept) with low blasts (<10% in chronic phase) and thrombocytosis is characteristic. Teardrop cells + leukoerythroblastic picture = myelofibrosis. Hypersegmented neutrophils = megaloblastic anemia. Basophilia is the key distinguishing feature of CML from leukemoid reactions (which have minimal basophilia).
highYield: CML blood film: full myeloid spectrum + absolute basophilia + thrombocytosis + low blast count. Basophilia ≥20% = accelerated phase criterion. Basophilia distinguishes CML from leukemoid reaction.
pyqNote: Basophilia as a characteristic of CML, its significance in accelerated phase, and its utility in differentiating CML from leukemoid reactions are commonly tested NEET PG points.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 72-year-old man has serum M-protein of 0.8 g/dL (IgG kappa), bone marrow plasma cells of 4%, no CRAB criteria, no lytic bone lesions, and no amyloidosis. Serum free light chain ratio is slightly abnormal. What is the most appropriate diagnosis and management?
options: Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS); observation/monitoring only
explanation: Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS) is defined by: (1) Serum M-protein <3 g/dL; (2) Bone marrow plasma cells <10%; (3) ABSENCE of CRAB criteria (no end-organ damage). MGUS is a premalignant condition that progresses to myeloma (or related disorders) at ~1% per year. Management is observation/monitoring (repeat SPEP in 6 months, then annually). Smoldering Myeloma (asymptomatic MM) = M-protein ≥3 g/dL OR plasma cells 10-60% WITHOUT CRAB. Active MM requires ≥60% plasma cells OR CRAB criteria OR biomarker criteria (serum free light chain ratio ≥100, MRI lesions). This patient meets MGUS criteria (M-protein <3g/dL, plasma cells <10%, no CRAB).
