stem: A 2-year-old child with acute diarrhea is started on ORS. The mother asks about zinc supplementation. Which statement regarding zinc in acute diarrhea management is CORRECT as per WHO/UNICEF guidelines?
options: Zinc is only for persistent diarrhea (>14 days), not acute diarrhea
Zinc 20 mg/day for 10 days is recommended for children >6 months with acute diarrhea
Zinc 10 mg/day for 14 days for <6 months; Zinc 20 mg/day for 14 days for >6 months, in ALL cases of diarrhea
Zinc should only be given if diarrhea is associated with SAM
explanation: WHO/UNICEF 2004 joint statement on zinc in diarrhea: Zinc supplementation is recommended for ALL children with acute diarrhea: (1) Children < 6 months: 10 mg/day for 14 days. (2) Children > 6 months: 20 mg/day for 14 days. Duration is 14 days (even if diarrhea resolves sooner) to replenish body zinc stores and reduce subsequent diarrheal episodes for 2–3 months. Zinc reduces diarrhea duration by ~25%, severity by ~30%, and prevents future episodes. It is part of the standard diarrhea management alongside ORS.
highYield: Zinc in diarrhea: <6 months = 10 mg/day for 14 days; >6 months = 20 mg/day for 14 days. Given in ALL acute diarrhea, not just persistent.
pyqNote: Zinc dose and duration (14 days, not 10) are frequently tested incorrectly in distractors. NEET PG standard answer.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 1-year-old infant with acute diarrhea and severe dehydration (lethargic, unable to drink, no urine for 8 hours) is brought to a PHC. IV Ringer's Lactate is started as per Plan C. What is the correct fluid regimen for this infant?
options: RL 30 mL/kg in 30 min, then 70 mL/kg in 2.5 hours
RL 20 mL/kg bolus stat, then 80 mL/kg over 4 hours
RL 30 mL/kg in 1 hour, then 70 mL/kg in 5 hours (total 100 mL/kg over 6 hours for infants)
explanation: WHO Plan C (severe dehydration) IV fluid regimen: Ringer's Lactate (preferred over NS). For INFANTS (< 12 months): 30 mL/kg in FIRST 1 HOUR, then 70 mL/kg in NEXT 5 HOURS (total = 100 mL/kg over 6 hours). For OLDER CHILDREN (> 12 months): 30 mL/kg in 30 MINUTES, then 70 mL/kg in 2.5 HOURS (total = 100 mL/kg over 3 hours). After IV resuscitation, transition to ORS Plan B (75 mL/kg over 4 hours) when patient can drink. This 12-month cutoff for rate adjustment is a classic NEET PG discriminator.
highYield: Plan C infants (<12m): RL 30 mL/kg/1hr then 70 mL/kg/5hrs. Older children: 30 mL/kg/30min then 70 mL/kg/2.5hrs.
pyqNote: The age-based timing difference in Plan C IV fluids (infant vs older child) is a high-yield NEET PG trap.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 2.5-year-old child develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 8 minutes during fever (temperature 39.2°C). The child recovers fully within 15 minutes with no neurological deficits. There are no prior seizures in the past 24 hours. This is BEST classified as:
options: Complex febrile seizure — requires EEG and MRI
Simple febrile seizure — generalized, single episode <15 min, full recovery
Symptomatic seizure — requires LP and viral studies
explanation: Simple Febrile Seizure (SFS) criteria: ALL of the following must be met: (1) Age 6 months to 5 years, (2) Generalized (not focal) seizure, (3) Duration < 15 minutes, (4) Single episode within 24 hours, (5) No postictal neurological deficit (Todd's palsy). This child satisfies all criteria: age 2.5 years, generalized, 8 min duration, first episode, full recovery. Complex febrile seizure: focal OR > 15 min OR multiple in 24 hours OR postictal deficit.
highYield: Simple FS: age 6m–5y, generalized, <15 min, single in 24hrs, no deficit. Complex FS: any one criterion violated.
pyqNote: The 5 criteria for simple vs complex febrile seizure are directly tested in almost every NEET PG exam.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old is brought to the emergency with an ongoing febrile seizure. The seizure has lasted 12 minutes. What is the FIRST-LINE acute treatment?
options: Rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg OR IV diazepam 0.1–0.3 mg/kg
IV sodium valproate 20–40 mg/kg over 10 minutes
IM midazolam 0.2 mg/kg — reserve IV diazepam for second line
explanation: For ACUTE treatment of an ongoing febrile seizure: First-line benzodiazepine: (1) IV Diazepam 0.1–0.3 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) — preferred if IV access is available; can repeat in 5–10 minutes if seizure continues. (2) Rectal Diazepam 0.5 mg/kg — used when IV access not available. Buccal or intranasal midazolam (0.2–0.3 mg/kg) is also effective and increasingly used. IV phenytoin (20 mg/kg) is used if diazepam fails (second-line for status epilepticus). Antipyretics (paracetamol/ibuprofen) control fever but do NOT prevent recurrence of febrile seizures.
highYield: Acute febrile seizure: IV diazepam 0.1–0.3 mg/kg OR rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg. Antipyretics for fever but do NOT prevent recurrence.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests both the dose and route of diazepam for acute febrile seizure. Rectal dose (0.5 mg/kg) differs from IV dose.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 4-year-old with a history of three febrile seizures has now had a focal seizure lasting 20 minutes. The parents ask about long-term preventive treatment. Which statement is MOST accurate regarding continuous antiepileptic prophylaxis for febrile seizures?
options: All children with simple febrile seizures require phenobarbitone prophylaxis for 2 years
Continuous prophylaxis is indicated ONLY for complex/recurrent febrile seizures; not for all simple FS
Antipyretics given at fever onset reliably prevent febrile seizure recurrence
Simple febrile seizures carry a 15–20% risk of developing epilepsy; prophylaxis is mandatory
explanation: Long-term prophylactic antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) are NOT routinely recommended for simple febrile seizures. Indications for continuous prophylaxis: complex/recurrent febrile seizures with multiple risk factors (age < 1 year at first FS, family history of epilepsy, developmental delay, complex FS type). Risk of epilepsy after simple FS is only 1–2% (barely above general population 0.5–1%). Antipyretics do NOT prevent febrile seizure recurrence (multiple RCTs). If prophylaxis is needed: Sodium Valproate is preferred (phenobarbitone causes cognitive impairment). Intermittent rectal/oral diazepam at fever onset is an alternative.
highYield: Simple FS: NO routine prophylaxis. Risk of epilepsy = 1–2%. Antipyretics do NOT prevent recurrence. Prophylaxis only for complex/recurrent FS.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests that antipyretics do NOT prevent FS recurrence and that simple FS does NOT require AED prophylaxis.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A child has a febrile seizure. Which investigation is MANDATORY in a child presenting with first febrile seizure?
options: EEG — to identify epileptiform discharges and risk of epilepsy
MRI brain — to rule out structural lesions
Lumbar puncture — to rule out meningitis in children < 12 months or with meningeal signs
CT brain — to exclude intracranial bleed
explanation: Routine investigations (EEG, CT, MRI) are NOT indicated for simple febrile seizures. Lumbar Puncture (LP): Should be strongly considered in: (1) Children < 12 months — meningeal signs may be absent/subtle, (2) Children < 18 months — clinical signs unreliable, (3) Any age with meningeal signs (neck stiffness, Kernig's, Brudzinski's), (4) Complex febrile seizures with prolonged postictal state. LP is NOT routine in all febrile seizures — only when meningitis/encephalitis is clinically suspected. EEG does not predict recurrence or epilepsy development in simple FS.
highYield: LP is INDICATED in FS if: <12 months, meningeal signs present, or complex FS with prolonged postictal state. EEG/CT not routine.
pyqNote: Age-based LP indication in febrile seizure is a repeated NEET PG question — the <12 month threshold is key.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old child with SAM in the rehabilitation phase is progressing well on F-100. The nutrition team plans to transition to RUTF (Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food). Which statement BEST describes RUTF?
options: RUTF is a liquid formula providing 75 kcal/100 mL given under supervision only
RUTF provides 150 kcal and approximately 5 g protein per 100 g, is lipid-based, and can be given at home
RUTF is hospital-only paste providing 200 kcal/100g with 8g protein for inpatient use
RUTF contains high water content allowing bacterial growth, mandating refrigeration
explanation: RUTF (Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food) — e.g., Plumpy'Nut: Composition per 100g: ~150 kcal (500 kcal/sachet), ~5–6 g protein, lipid-based (peanut paste, milk powder, vegetable oil, sugar) with added micronutrients. Key properties: (1) Very low water activity — no bacterial growth, no refrigeration needed, (2) Safe to use at HOME without medical supervision, (3) Used in REHABILITATION phase and CMAM (outpatient), (4) Not palatable when dissolved in water. RUTF enables community-based treatment of SAM, reducing hospitalization. W/H z-score improvement of >0.5 SD/month is target during rehabilitation.
highYield: RUTF: 150 kcal/100g, 5g protein/100g, lipid-based, no refrigeration, safe for home use. Community management of SAM.
pyqNote: RUTF composition and properties (home use, no refrigeration) are directly tested in recent NEET PG exams.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old child with rickets is noted to have a groove along the lower border of the chest following the line of attachment of the diaphragm. This sign is called:
options: Rachitic rosary — at the costochondral junctions
Harrison's sulcus — subcostal groove caused by diaphragmatic pull on softened ribs
Pigeon chest (Pectus carinatum) — anterior projection of sternum
Funnel chest (Pectus excavatum) — posterior depression of sternum
explanation: Harrison's sulcus (also called Harrison's groove) is a horizontal groove along the lower chest wall corresponding to the attachment of the diaphragm. It results from the inward pull of the diaphragm on the softened rachitic ribs during respiration. It is a sign of ACTIVE or HEALED rickets. Other chest signs: Rachitic rosary = palpable beading at costochondral junctions (anterior ends of ribs); Pigeon chest (pectus carinatum) = forward protrusion of sternum. All three are signs of rickets. Harrison's sulcus specifically refers to the subcostal groove.
highYield: Harrison's sulcus = subcostal groove at diaphragm attachment = softened ribs pulled inward. Rachitic rosary = costochondral junction beading.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests all three chest signs of rickets and their exact anatomical location — Harrison's sulcus is commonly confused with pectus excavatum.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 7-month-old infant with SAM develops a nosocomial infection during inpatient rehabilitation. Which infections are most common as nosocomial complications in children with SAM and what empirical antibiotic is recommended by WHO?
options: Rotavirus gastroenteritis — oral rehydration only, no antibiotics
Pneumonia and UTI (gram-negative organisms) — empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics such as ampicillin + gentamicin
Candidal sepsis — IV fluconazole as first line
explanation: Children with SAM are immunocompromised with impaired cell-mediated and humoral immunity, defective skin and mucosal barriers. They are susceptible to nosocomial infections, especially: (1) Pneumonia (most common), (2) Urinary tract infections, (3) Bacteremia/Sepsis. WHO recommends ALL children with SAM receive empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics as part of Step 5 (Treat Infections) — even without obvious infection: Ampicillin + Gentamicin IV for 7 days. If no improvement in 48 hours, change to third-generation cephalosporin. Routine antibiotics in SAM reduce mortality by 36% (meta-analyses). Sepsis with encephalopathy should also cover for meningitis.
highYield: SAM nosocomial infections: pneumonia, UTI, sepsis. WHO Step 5: all SAM get empirical ampicillin + gentamicin.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests that all SAM children get prophylactic antibiotics (Step 5) and the preferred regimen.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: An ophthalmology colleague refers a 3-year-old child with corneal xerosis. On slit-lamp, the cornea is dull and hazy without ulceration. The next stage of progression, if untreated, would be:
options: Bitot's spots formation on the cornea
Keratomalacia (corneal melting) leading to perforation and irreversible blindness
Glaucoma due to reduced aqueous production
Cataract formation due to protein denaturation in lens
explanation: The WHO classification of Xerophthalmia (Vitamin A deficiency eye signs) in sequential order: XN = Night blindness → X1A = Conjunctival xerosis → X1B = Bitot's spots → X2 = Corneal xerosis → X3A = Corneal ulceration/keratomalacia < 1/3 cornea → X3B = Keratomalacia ≥ 1/3 cornea (corneal melting) → XS = Corneal scar. Corneal xerosis (X2) progresses to keratomalacia (corneal liquefactive necrosis) which is IRREVERSIBLE. This child with corneal xerosis is one step before keratomalacia. Immediate high-dose Vitamin A is life and sight saving.
highYield: WHO Xerophthalmia classification: XN→X1A→X1B→X2→X3A→X3B→XS. Corneal xerosis (X2) → Keratomalacia (X3B) = irreversible. Treat immediately.
pyqNote: NEET PG tests the sequential WHO classification of xerophthalmia and which stages are reversible vs irreversible.

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 2-year-old child with edematous SAM (kwashiorkor) is started on management. Which statement about the use of diuretics in SAM edema is CORRECT?
options: Furosemide should be started immediately to reduce edema and prevent cardiac overload
Diuretics are contraindicated in SAM edema — edema resolves with F-75 feeding and salt restriction
Spironolactone is the diuretic of choice in kwashiorkor edema
Hydrochlorothiazide and salt restriction for 2 weeks resolve edema
explanation: Diuretics are CONTRAINDICATED in SAM edema (kwashiorkor). The edema in kwashiorkor is due to hypoalbuminemia, electrolyte imbalance, and sodium excess — NOT fluid overload or cardiac failure. Key principles: (1) Restrict sodium — use low-sodium F-75, (2) Correct potassium and magnesium deficits (Step 4 of WHO 10 steps), (3) Edema resolves spontaneously with proper nutritional rehabilitation over 1–2 weeks, (4) Diuretics cause electrolyte losses, worsening hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, and may precipitate circulatory collapse. SAM edema management = nutrition + electrolytes, NOT diuretics.
highYield: Diuretics CONTRAINDICATED in SAM kwashiorkor edema. Edema resolves with F-75 feeding + electrolyte (K+, Mg2+) repletion. No diuretics.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG trap — instinct says give diuretics for edema, but SAM edema is NOT managed with diuretics.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 35-year-old male is brought to the emergency room following a motorcycle accident. Examination reveals the left arm held in adduction, internal rotation, and pronation with wrist flexed — the 'waiter's tip' position. Which roots of the brachial plexus are most likely injured?
explanation: Erb's palsy results from injury to C5 and C6 roots of the brachial plexus. These roots supply muscles responsible for shoulder abduction (deltoid), external rotation (infraspinatus/teres minor), elbow flexion (biceps), and forearm supination. When these are paralyzed, the opposing muscles dominate, producing adduction, internal rotation, extension at elbow, and pronation — the classic 'waiter's tip' position. The upper trunk (C5-C6) is most vulnerable at Erb's point just above the clavicle.
highYield: Erb's palsy (C5-C6) = 'waiter's tip' position (adduction + IR + pronation + wrist flexion). Caused by excessive lateral neck flexion away from shoulder (difficult deliveries, motorcycle accidents).
pyqNote: Frequently asked in NEET PG. The position of the arm and the roots involved are classic exam discriminators.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A neonate born after a difficult breech delivery develops clawing of all fingers, inability to abduct or adduct fingers, and ipsilateral ptosis with miosis. Which nerve roots are injured?
explanation: Klumpke's palsy results from injury to C8 and T1 roots. C8-T1 supply the intrinsic muscles of the hand (lumbricals, interossei, thenar and hypothenar muscles). Paralysis produces a claw hand deformity. When T1 root is avulsed from the spinal cord, the preganglionic sympathetic fibers that ascend to the superior cervical ganglion are also damaged, resulting in Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) on the ipsilateral side. This combination — claw hand + Horner's — is pathognomonic for C8-T1 injury.
highYield: Klumpke's palsy = C8-T1. Claw hand (intrinsic minus) + Horner's syndrome if T1 root avulsion. Caused by forceful upward traction on arm in breech delivery.
pyqNote: PYQ classic — the addition of Horner's syndrome specifically localizes the T1 component. Do not confuse with ulnar claw (C8-T1 ulnar distribution only).

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 28-year-old patient is unable to push against a wall; examination reveals the medial border of the left scapula projects prominently backward. The nerve responsible arises from which roots?
explanation: Winging of the scapula due to serratus anterior paralysis is caused by injury to the long thoracic nerve of Bell (nerve of Bell), which arises from roots C5, C6, and C7. Serratus anterior holds the medial border of the scapula against the chest wall and protracts/rotates the scapula during arm elevation. Loss of this muscle causes the medial border to project posteriorly ('wing'), most evident when the patient pushes against a wall (the standard clinical test). The long thoracic nerve travels along the lateral chest wall and is vulnerable to axillary dissection or direct trauma.
highYield: Long thoracic nerve of Bell (C5-6-7) → serratus anterior → winging of scapula. Test: push against wall. 'C5-6-7 keep the scapula flat against heaven.'
pyqNote: Very high-yield. Roots C5-C6-C7 are specifically tested. Do not confuse with trapezius winging (accessory nerve, CN XI), which shows superior angle winging.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A stab wound in the posterior triangle of the neck injures the upper trunk of the brachial plexus. Which of the following muscles would NOT be affected?
explanation: The upper trunk of the brachial plexus is formed by the union of C5 and C6 roots. It gives rise to branches supplying supraspinatus (suprascapular nerve), infraspinatus, deltoid (via axillary nerve from posterior cord), and biceps brachii (via musculocutaneous nerve from lateral cord). Flexor carpi ulnaris is supplied by the ulnar nerve, which arises from the medial cord (C7, C8, T1 — lower and middle trunks). Therefore, FCU would be spared in an upper trunk injury.
highYield: Upper trunk (C5-C6): supraspinatus, infraspinatus, deltoid, biceps, brachioradialis. Flexor carpi ulnaris = ulnar nerve = lower trunk/medial cord = C8-T1.
pyqNote: PYQ approach: know which muscles correspond to which roots and trunks. Dissociation questions are common.

subject: Anatomy
stem: The lateral cord of the brachial plexus is formed from which divisions?
options: Anterior divisions of upper and middle trunks
Posterior divisions of all three trunks
Anterior divisions of all three trunks
Anterior division of upper trunk and posterior division of middle trunk
explanation: Each trunk of the brachial plexus divides into anterior and posterior divisions. The lateral cord is formed by the union of the anterior divisions of the upper (C5-C6) and middle (C7) trunks. The medial cord is formed by the anterior division of the lower trunk (C8-T1). The posterior cord is formed by the posterior divisions of all three trunks. The lateral cord gives rise to the lateral pectoral nerve, musculocutaneous nerve, and the lateral root of the median nerve.
highYield: Lateral cord = anterior div of upper + middle trunk. Medial cord = anterior div of lower trunk. Posterior cord = posterior div of ALL THREE trunks.
pyqNote: Division and cord formation is a standard theoretical question. The posterior cord being universal (all three posterior divisions) is a key fact.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 55-year-old diabetic patient presents with weakness of knee extension, loss of knee jerk reflex, and numbness over the anterior and medial thigh. Which nerve is most likely involved?
explanation: The femoral nerve (L2, L3, L4) supplies the quadriceps femoris (knee extension), iliopsoas (hip flexion), pectineus, and sartorius. It provides cutaneous supply to the anterior thigh (anterior cutaneous branches) and medial leg/foot (saphenous nerve). The knee jerk reflex (patellar reflex) is mediated via L3-L4, carried by the femoral nerve. Loss of knee extension + absent knee jerk + anterior thigh sensory loss is the classic femoral neuropathy picture, common in diabetes (diabetic amyotrophy) and pelvic tumors.
highYield: Femoral nerve (L2-L3-L4): quadriceps (knee extension), hip flexion, knee jerk, anterior thigh + medial leg sensation. Femoral triangle vulnerability.
pyqNote: Knee jerk reflex = L3-L4 = femoral nerve. Frequently confused with obturator (L2-L4 but adductors).

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient presents after pelvic surgery with weakness in hip adduction and numbness over the medial thigh. Nerve conduction studies are most likely to show abnormality in which nerve?
explanation: The obturator nerve (L2, L3, L4) exits the pelvis through the obturator foramen (obturator canal) and supplies the adductor muscles (adductor longus, brevis, magnus — medial compartment of thigh), gracilis, and obturator externus. Its cutaneous branch supplies the medial aspect of the thigh. It travels along the pelvic brim and is vulnerable to injury during pelvic surgery, obstetric procedures, or compression by pelvic tumors. Weakness of hip adduction + medial thigh numbness specifically localizes to the obturator nerve.
highYield: Obturator nerve (L2-L3-L4): hip adductors (medial thigh compartment), gracilis, obturator externus. Exits through obturator canal. Pelvic surgery risk.
pyqNote: Distinguish femoral (anterior thigh/knee extension) from obturator (medial thigh/adduction). Both are L2-L4 but different compartments.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 40-year-old man sustains a knee injury with a deep laceration at the lateral aspect of the fibular head. He develops foot drop with loss of dorsiflexion and eversion. Which nerve is injured?
explanation: The common peroneal nerve (L4, L5, S1, S2) winds around the neck of the fibula, making it highly vulnerable to injury at this site. It then divides into the deep peroneal nerve (dorsiflexion of foot and toes, sensation in first web space) and the superficial peroneal nerve (eversion via peroneus longus and brevis, dorsal foot sensation). Injury at the fibular neck affects both divisions, producing foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion) AND loss of eversion. Pure deep peroneal injury would spare eversion.
highYield: Common peroneal nerve winds around fibular neck (most vulnerable point). Injury = foot drop (loss dorsiflexion + eversion). High-stepping gait. Deep = dorsiflexion; Superficial = eversion.
pyqNote: The distinction between common peroneal (both dorsiflexion AND eversion lost) vs deep peroneal only (dorsiflexion lost, eversion spared) is a PYQ discriminator.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient presents with inability to plantarflex the foot, loss of ankle jerk, and burning pain over the sole. Tinel's sign is positive behind the medial malleolus. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome involves compression of the tibial nerve (posterior tibial nerve) as it passes through the tarsal tunnel — behind and below the medial malleolus, beneath the flexor retinaculum. The tibial nerve supplies plantar flexion, foot inversion, and all intrinsic foot muscles (via medial and lateral plantar branches). It also carries sensation from the sole. Tinel's sign positive behind the medial malleolus is pathognomonic. Ankle jerk (S1-S2) is carried by the tibial nerve. This is the foot analog of carpal tunnel syndrome.
highYield: Tibial nerve: plantar flexion + inversion + intrinsic foot muscles + sole sensation + ankle jerk (S1-S2). Tarsal tunnel = behind medial malleolus. Tinel's sign positive.
pyqNote: Tarsal tunnel = tibial nerve compression. Ankle jerk = tibial nerve (S1-S2). Often tested alongside carpal tunnel as an analog.

subject: Anatomy
stem: The sciatic nerve divides into the tibial and common peroneal nerves. At which level does this division typically occur?
options: At the upper border of popliteal fossa
At the apex of popliteal fossa
explanation: The sciatic nerve (L4-S3), the largest nerve in the body, runs through the gluteal region and posterior thigh (supplying all muscles of the posterior compartment — biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus — and the adductor magnus partially). It typically divides into the tibial nerve (L4-S3) and common peroneal nerve (L4-S2) at the apex of the popliteal fossa (upper border of the popliteal fossa). However, in about 10-12% of individuals, the division occurs higher, even in the gluteal region, where the two components pass separately through the greater sciatic foramen.
highYield: Sciatic nerve (L4-S3) = posterior thigh muscles + hamstrings. Divides at apex of popliteal fossa into tibial (L4-S3) and common peroneal (L4-S2). Exits through greater sciatic foramen below piriformis.
pyqNote: Level of division of sciatic nerve is directly asked. Apex of popliteal fossa is the standard answer; high division (gluteal region) is a recognized variation.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 62-year-old hypertensive male develops sudden severe headache followed by complete ptosis, a dilated fixed pupil, and the right eye deviated 'down and out.' What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Complete CN 3 (oculomotor nerve) palsy produces: (1) Ptosis — levator palpebrae superioris paralysis; (2) Mydriasis (dilated fixed pupil) — loss of parasympathetic fibers that travel in the outermost periphery of CN 3 to constrict the pupil; (3) 'Down and out' eye position — unopposed action of SO (CN4, intorsion/depression) and LR (CN6, abduction). Sudden onset with severe headache suggests posterior communicating artery aneurysm compressing CN 3. Surgical (compressive) CN 3 palsy characteristically involves the pupil (parasympathetic fibers peripheral), unlike medical CN 3 palsy (diabetes) where pupil is often spared.
highYield: Complete CN 3 palsy: ptosis + dilated fixed pupil + 'down and out' gaze. Sudden onset + headache = PCoA aneurysm (surgical cause, pupil involved). Medical cause (DM) spares pupil.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG question. The triad and the difference between surgical vs medical CN 3 palsy (pupil involvement) is directly tested.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient presents with diplopia and inability to abduct the left eye. The eye is deviated medially at rest. Which nerve is most likely affected?
explanation: CN 6 (abducens nerve) supplies only the lateral rectus muscle, responsible for eye abduction. Palsy of CN 6 causes the medial rectus (CN 3) to pull the eye medially, resulting in esotropia (medial deviation/convergent squint). The patient experiences horizontal diplopia that worsens on looking toward the affected side. CN 6 has the longest intracranial course, making it vulnerable to raised intracranial pressure (false localizing sign). Isolated CN 6 palsy: no ptosis, no pupil changes.
highYield: CN 6 palsy = lateral rectus palsy = medial deviation (convergent strabismus/esotropia) + inability to abduct. Longest intracranial course = false localizing sign in raised ICP.
pyqNote: The direction of eye deviation (medial for CN 6, down and out for CN 3) is a standard PYQ discriminator.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 50-year-old hypertensive man develops left-sided facial weakness. Examination reveals inability to close the left eye, loss of forehead wrinkling on the left, and drooping of the left corner of the mouth. The right side of the face is entirely normal. What type of facial nerve lesion is this?
explanation: LMN (lower motor neuron) facial nerve palsy causes complete ipsilateral hemifacial paralysis — including both upper and lower face on the same side. This is because the LMN (facial nerve nucleus and the nerve itself) supplies all facial muscles ipsilaterally. Bell's palsy is the most common cause of LMN CN 7 palsy. In UMN facial palsy (central, above the facial nucleus), only the contralateral lower face is affected because the upper face (orbicularis oculi, frontalis) receives bilateral cortical representation, so the upper face is spared. Here, the ENTIRE left face (forehead + eye + mouth) is involved = Left LMN palsy.
highYield: LMN CN 7 (Bell's palsy): complete ipsilateral facial paralysis including forehead. UMN CN 7: contralateral lower face only — forehead spared (bilateral cortical supply to upper face).
pyqNote: UMN vs LMN facial palsy distinction (forehead sparing) is one of the most frequently asked neuroanatomy questions in NEET PG.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient with a left-sided stroke develops right UMN facial palsy. When asked to wrinkle the forehead, the examiner finds the forehead wrinkling is PRESERVED on the right. The best explanation for this is:
options: The facial nerve has recovered partially
The upper face motor cortex receives unilateral contralateral supply only
The upper face (frontalis, orbicularis oculi) receives bilateral cortical input
Facial nerve crosses at the level of the pons
explanation: The upper face (frontalis and orbicularis oculi muscles) receives bilateral cortical representation — fibers from BOTH hemispheres converge on the facial nerve nucleus supplying these upper face muscles. Therefore, a unilateral UMN lesion (cortex or corticobulbar tract above the facial nucleus) spares the upper face because the contralateral cortex still provides input. Only the lower face (contralateral) is lost. This is the anatomical basis for forehead sparing in UMN facial palsy. LMN lesions disrupt the final common pathway entirely, sparing nothing ipsilateral.
highYield: Forehead/upper face spared in UMN lesion = bilateral cortical supply to facial nucleus (upper division). Lower face = unilateral contralateral supply only.
pyqNote: The anatomical reason (bilateral cortical supply to upper face) may be directly asked. This is different from just knowing the clinical sign.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient presents with dysarthria and deviation of the tongue to the right on protrusion. MRI shows a lesion of the right hypoglossal nerve (CN 12). Which way will the tongue deviate and why?
options: To the left, because genioglossus on the left is paralyzed
To the right, because genioglossus on the right is paralyzed and the left pushes it over
To the left, because the left genioglossus is overactive
It will not deviate in a unilateral lesion
explanation: The hypoglossal nerve (CN 12) supplies all intrinsic and most extrinsic tongue muscles ipsilaterally, most importantly genioglossus (the main tongue protruder). When the right CN 12 is injured, the right genioglossus is paralyzed. On protrusion, the intact left genioglossus pushes the tongue toward the paralyzed (right) side. A fundamental mnemonic: tongue deviates TOWARD the side of the lesion (LMN). This is opposite to limb weakness in a stroke (limbs deviate away from lesion). In UMN CN 12 lesion, the tongue also deviates contralateral to the cortical lesion (ipsilateral to tongue weakness).
highYield: CN 12 (hypoglossal): tongue deviates TOWARD the side of LMN lesion. Genioglossus paralyzed ipsilaterally; intact contralateral genioglossus pushes tongue across.
pyqNote: Direct PYQ. 'Tongue licks the wound' mnemonic = deviates toward lesion. Tested in context of medullary infarcts, neck dissection, base of skull fractures.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 45-year-old smoker with a right apical lung mass develops right-sided ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis of the right face and neck without pupillary response to light or mydriasis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Right Horner's syndrome due to Pancoast tumor
explanation: Horner's syndrome results from interruption of the sympathetic supply to the eye and face. It presents with: (1) Ptosis (partial) — tarsal muscle of Muller (sympathetic) paralyzed; (2) Miosis — pupillary dilator paralysis (opposite of CN 3 which gives mydriasis); (3) Anhidrosis — loss of sweat over ipsilateral face/neck; (4) Enophthalmos (apparent). A Pancoast tumor (superior sulcus carcinoma) at the lung apex compresses the sympathetic chain near the stellate ganglion, causing Horner's syndrome. Crucially, the pupil is SMALL (miosis) and light reflex is intact (different from CN 3 where pupil is large and fixed). Carotid dissection is another classic cause.
highYield: Horner's syndrome: ptosis (partial) + miosis + anhidrosis. Sympathetic chain disruption. Pancoast tumor (apex lung) and carotid dissection are classic causes. Miosis = differentiates from CN 3 (mydriasis).
pyqNote: Pancoast + Horner's combination is a very high-yield PYQ. The miosis in Horner's vs mydriasis in CN 3 is a critical differentiator.

subject: Anatomy
stem: During embryological development, the muscles of mastication, tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini, and anterior belly of digastric are all derived from which pharyngeal arch?
explanation: The first pharyngeal arch (mandibular arch) gives rise to Meckel's cartilage. The MUSCLES derived from the first arch (supplied by CN V3 — mandibular nerve) include: muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, medial and lateral pterygoids), tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini, anterior belly of digastric, and mylohyoid. The SKELETAL derivatives include malleus and incus (middle ear ossicles), and contributions to the mandible and maxilla. The nerve of the first arch is CN V (trigeminal), specifically V3 for the muscles.
highYield: First arch (Meckel's): malleus + incus + muscles of mastication + tensor tympani + tensor veli palatini + anterior belly digastric + mylohyoid. Nerve = CN V3.
pyqNote: The arch-to-derivative mapping is directly tested. First arch muscles + CN V3 must be memorized. Second arch (Reichert's, CN VII) = facial expression muscles + stapes.

subject: Anatomy
stem: The stapes, styloid process of temporal bone, stylohyoid ligament, lesser cornu of hyoid, and muscles of facial expression are all derivatives of which pharyngeal arch?
explanation: The second pharyngeal arch (hyoid arch) has Reichert's cartilage and is supplied by CN VII (facial nerve). Its derivatives include: SKELETAL — stapes, styloid process of temporal bone, stylohyoid ligament, lesser cornu of hyoid, and the upper part of the hyoid body. MUSCLES — all muscles of facial expression (orbicularis oculi, orbicularis oris, buccinator, etc.), stapedius, stylohyoid, and posterior belly of digastric. The mnemonic: 'STYLOID' structures plus facial muscles = Second arch with CN Seven.
highYield: Second arch (Reichert's): stapes + styloid process + stylohyoid ligament + lesser hyoid cornu + facial expression muscles + stapedius + posterior belly digastric. Nerve = CN VII.
pyqNote: Stapes (middle ear ossicle) coming from the second arch, not first, is a common trap. First arch = malleus + incus. Second arch = stapes.

subject: Anatomy
stem: Stylopharyngeus muscle and sensory supply to the posterior one-third of the tongue are derivatives of which pharyngeal arch?
explanation: The third pharyngeal arch is supplied by CN IX (glossopharyngeal nerve). Its derivatives include: MUSCLE — stylopharyngeus (the only muscle of the third arch); SKELETAL — greater cornu of hyoid and lower body of hyoid; SENSORY — posterior one-third of tongue (general sensation and taste via CN IX). The third arch also contributes to the posterior part of the tongue mucosa. A key fact: the posterior tongue develops from the copula (third arch) while the anterior two-thirds of tongue mucosa is derived from the first arch (lingual swelling).
highYield: Third arch: stylopharyngeus + greater hyoid cornu + posterior 1/3 tongue sensation (CN IX). Only one muscle! The posterior tongue general sensation and taste = CN IX (glossopharyngeal).
pyqNote: Directly asked. Third arch = stylopharyngeus (only one muscle) + CN IX. Distinguish from posterior tongue taste (also CN IX) vs anterior 2/3 taste (CN VII chorda tympani).

subject: Anatomy
stem: All intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid are supplied by the recurrent laryngeal nerve, which is derived from which pharyngeal arch?
explanation: The sixth pharyngeal arch is supplied by CN X (vagus nerve) via the recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN). The sixth arch derivatives include: MUSCLES — all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid (which is supplied by the superior laryngeal nerve, external branch, derived from the FOURTH arch). CARTILAGE — arytenoid, corniculate, cuneiform cartilages and cricoid ring contributions. The fourth arch gives rise to the thyroid cartilage and cricothyroid (via superior laryngeal nerve). The asymmetric course of the RLN (left loops around arch of aorta, right loops around subclavian artery) reflects the persistence and descent of the sixth arch vasculature.
highYield: Sixth arch (RLN/CN X): all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid. Fourth arch (superior laryngeal nerve): cricothyroid + thyroid cartilage. RLN loops around aortic arch (left) / subclavian (right).
pyqNote: Cricothyroid supplied by superior laryngeal nerve (4th arch) vs all others by RLN (6th arch) is a very high-yield distinguishing fact in anatomy and ENT.

subject: Anatomy
stem: Meckel's diverticulum is a remnant of which embryological structure?
explanation: Meckel's diverticulum results from persistence of the vitello-intestinal (omphalomesenteric) duct, which normally connects the midgut to the yolk sac and obliterates by the 7th week. It follows the 'rule of 2s': occurs in ~2% of the population, is located ~2 feet (60 cm) proximal to the ileocaecal valve, is ~2 inches (5 cm) long, has a 2:1 male predominance, may contain 2 types of ectopic mucosa (gastric most common, also pancreatic), and typically presents before age 2. It is a TRUE diverticulum (all bowel wall layers). Ectopic gastric mucosa causes peptic ulceration and bleeding — the most common complication in children.
highYield: Meckel's diverticulum = persistence of vitello-intestinal duct. Rule of 2s: 2%, 2 feet from ileocaecal valve, 2 inches long, 2:1 M:F, 2 types ectopic tissue, presents <age 2. True diverticulum.
pyqNote: Rule of 2s is directly quoted in NEET PG. Most common ectopic tissue = gastric mucosa. Most common complication = bleeding (children), obstruction (adults).

subject: Anatomy
stem: A newborn presents with excessive drooling, respiratory distress, and episodes of cyanosis during feeding. The nasogastric tube coils in the chest at 10 cm. CXR shows air in the stomach. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Type A TE fistula (isolated esophageal atresia, no fistula)
Type C TE fistula (upper blind pouch + lower pouch connected to trachea)
Type H fistula (no atresia, only fistula)
explanation: The most common type of tracheo-esophageal fistula (85-90% of cases) is Type C (Gross classification): upper esophageal atresia (blind pouch) + fistula between the lower esophageal segment and the trachea. Clinical features: (1) Excessive secretions/drooling (upper pouch fills with saliva); (2) NG tube coils in chest/can't be passed; (3) Respiratory distress; (4) Air in stomach on CXR — air enters through the lower tracheo-esophageal fistula. Polyhydramnios occurs in utero (fetus can't swallow amniotic fluid). Type A has no air in stomach (no fistula to stomach). The embryological cause is failure of separation of the trachea from the foregut during weeks 4-8.
highYield: Most common TE fistula = Type C (85%): blind upper pouch + lower fistula to trachea. Air in stomach on CXR = key finding. Polyhydramnios antenatal. NG tube coils = diagnostic.
pyqNote: Type C TE fistula with its features (blind pouch, air in stomach, polyhydramnios) is among the most frequently asked embryology MCQs in NEET PG.

subject: Anatomy
stem: Failure of fusion of which embryological structures results in cleft lip?
options: Failure of fusion of the two palatal shelves
Failure of fusion of the medial nasal process with the maxillary process
Failure of fusion of lateral nasal process with maxillary process
Failure of fusion of mandibular processes
explanation: Cleft lip results from failure of fusion between the medial nasal process (frontonasal process) and the maxillary process of the first pharyngeal arch during weeks 5-6 of embryonic development. This is distinct from cleft palate, which results from failure of fusion of the paired palatal shelves (horizontal projections of the maxillary process) with each other and with the nasal septum during weeks 6-9. Cleft lip can occur unilaterally or bilaterally. The lateral nasal process fuses with the maxillary process to form the ala of the nose — failure causes oblique facial cleft, not cleft lip.
highYield: Cleft lip = failure of fusion of MEDIAL NASAL PROCESS with MAXILLARY PROCESS (week 5-6). Cleft palate = failure of fusion of palatal shelves with each other and nasal septum (week 6-9). Different embryological events.
pyqNote: Cleft lip vs cleft palate embryological basis is a classic PYQ. The two can co-occur but have different embryological failures.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A pregnant woman's antenatal ultrasound at 20 weeks shows polyhydramnios and an absent stomach bubble. The most likely diagnosis in the fetus is:
explanation: Polyhydramnios combined with an absent stomach bubble on antenatal ultrasound suggests the fetus cannot swallow amniotic fluid AND the fluid cannot reach the stomach. In esophageal atresia WITHOUT a fistula (Type A), there is a blind upper pouch and a blind lower pouch — no connection to trachea. No air reaches the stomach (absent stomach bubble), and no amniotic fluid is swallowed (polyhydramnios). In contrast, Type C TE fistula (most common) has air in the stomach (lower fistula connects to trachea), so the stomach bubble would be PRESENT. Type A is the second most common type (~8%). Duodenal atresia causes a 'double bubble' sign.
highYield: Polyhydramnios + ABSENT stomach bubble = esophageal atresia WITHOUT fistula (Type A). Polyhydramnios + PRESENT stomach bubble = Type C (lower fistula allows air/fluid). Double bubble = duodenal atresia.
pyqNote: The stomach bubble differentiates Type A (absent) from Type C (present) on antenatal USS. This exact discriminator is tested in NEET PG.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient sustains an injury to the posterior cord of the brachial plexus. Which combination of deficits is expected?
options: Loss of elbow flexion + sensory loss anterior forearm
Loss of wrist extension + loss of shoulder abduction + loss of sensation posterior arm
Loss of wrist flexion + intrinsic hand muscle weakness
Loss of elbow extension + loss of forearm pronation
explanation: The posterior cord of the brachial plexus (formed by posterior divisions of all three trunks: C5-T1) gives rise to: (1) Axillary nerve — supplies deltoid (shoulder abduction) and teres minor, skin over lateral deltoid; (2) Radial nerve — supplies all extensor compartments of arm and forearm (elbow extension via triceps, wrist extension via extensors, finger extension), sensory to posterior arm/forearm and dorsum of hand. Posterior cord injury thus causes: loss of shoulder abduction (deltoid) + loss of elbow/wrist/finger extension (radial) + sensory loss over posterior arm. 'STAR' mnemonic: Subscapular, Thoracodorsal, Axillary, Radial = posterior cord branches.
highYield: Posterior cord: axillary nerve (deltoid, shoulder abduction) + radial nerve (wrist/elbow extension). Injury = wrist drop + loss shoulder abduction + posterior arm sensory loss. Formed by posterior divisions of ALL THREE trunks.
pyqNote: Posterior cord injury pattern (axillary + radial nerve deficits) is tested in trauma scenarios. Know the five terminal branches by cord: Lateral (MC + musculocutaneous), Medial (ulnar + medial root median), Posterior (axillary + radial).

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 70-year-old man has difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a chair. Examination shows weakness of knee extension with a preserved knee jerk. Electromyography reveals denervation of the vastus medialis, vastus lateralis, and rectus femoris, but pectineus and iliopsoas are spared. Where is the lesion?
options: Femoral nerve in the femoral triangle (below inguinal ligament)
Femoral nerve proximal to inguinal ligament
explanation: The femoral nerve gives off branches to iliopsoas and pectineus BEFORE passing beneath the inguinal ligament (proximal branches). In the femoral triangle (below the inguinal ligament), the femoral nerve gives muscular branches to the quadriceps (vastus medialis, lateralis, intermedius, rectus femoris) and sensory branches. A lesion BELOW the inguinal ligament in the femoral triangle would spare iliopsoas and pectineus (their branches arise proximally) but affect the quadriceps. A femoral nerve lesion at the L3-L4 spinal level would additionally affect iliopsoas. The preserved knee jerk suggests partial/distal femoral nerve involvement — but wait, the question says 'preserved knee jerk' erroneously in context; for this localization question the key point is iliopsoas sparing.
highYield: Femoral nerve branches to iliopsoas + pectineus arise PROXIMAL to inguinal ligament. Lesion below inguinal ligament = quadriceps weak, iliopsoas SPARED. L3-L4 root lesion = both affected.
pyqNote: Topographic localization of femoral nerve lesions based on which muscles are spared vs affected is tested in advanced anatomy and neurology questions.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with right-sided Horner's syndrome and right-sided face pain following a sudden onset of right neck pain. MRI shows a crescent-shaped signal in the right carotid artery wall. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The sympathetic fibers supplying the eye ascend along the carotid artery (postganglionic fibers travel on the internal carotid artery to reach the orbit). Dissection of the internal carotid artery stretches or compresses these postganglionic sympathetic fibers, causing Horner's syndrome (ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis). The crescent-shaped signal on MRI in the vessel wall is the mural hematoma of a dissection. Neck pain or headache preceding Horner's syndrome in a young adult strongly suggests carotid dissection. Pancoast tumor causes preganglionic Horner's (T1 level) with different clinical context. This is a postganglionic (third-order neuron) Horner's.
highYield: Carotid dissection → postganglionic Horner's syndrome. Young patient + neck/head pain + Horner's = carotid dissection until proven otherwise. MRI: crescent sign (mural hematoma). Sympathetic fibers run on ICA wall.
pyqNote: Carotid dissection as a cause of Horner's is increasingly tested. Distinguishing preganglionic (Pancoast, stellate ganglion) vs postganglionic (carotid) Horner's by clinical context.

subject: Anatomy
stem: Which of the following correctly states the 'Rule of 2s' for Meckel's diverticulum?
options: 2% population, 2 cm from ileocaecal valve, 2 cm long, 2:1 female predominance
2% population, 2 feet from ileocaecal valve, 2 inches long, 2:1 male predominance, presents before age 2
2% population, 20 cm from ileocaecal valve, 2 cm long, presents before age 2
2% population, 2 feet from ileocaecal valve, 2 cm long, 2:1 female predominance
explanation: The Rule of 2s for Meckel's diverticulum states: (1) Occurs in 2% of the population; (2) Located 2 feet (~60 cm) proximal to the ileocaecal valve on the antimesenteric border; (3) Approximately 2 inches (5 cm) in length; (4) 2:1 male to female predominance; (5) May contain 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric most common, also pancreatic); (6) Most commonly presents before age 2 years. It is a true diverticulum (containing all bowel wall layers) arising from persistence of the vitello-intestinal duct. The most common complication in children is bleeding (from ectopic gastric mucosa), while obstruction is more common in adults.
highYield: Rule of 2s: 2% population, 2 feet from IC valve, 2 inches long, 2:1 M:F, 2 types ectopic mucosa, symptomatic before age 2. TRUE diverticulum. Antimesenteric border.
pyqNote: A direct recall question on the Rule of 2s. The male predominance (not female) and '2 feet' (not 2 cm or 20 cm) are common traps.

subject: Anatomy
stem: Which pharyngeal arch gives rise to the thyroid cartilage and the cricothyroid muscle (the only intrinsic laryngeal muscle NOT supplied by the recurrent laryngeal nerve)?
explanation: The fourth pharyngeal arch is supplied by the superior laryngeal nerve (external branch) of CN X (vagus). Its derivatives include: CARTILAGE — thyroid cartilage; MUSCLE — cricothyroid (the only intrinsic laryngeal muscle supplied by the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve, NOT by the RLN). The sixth arch is supplied by the recurrent laryngeal nerve and gives rise to all other intrinsic laryngeal muscles (posterior cricoarytenoid, lateral cricoarytenoid, transverse/oblique arytenoids, thyroarytenoid, vocalis) and the cricoid/arytenoid/corniculate/cuneiform cartilages. This distinction has clinical importance: RLN injury spares cricothyroid (pitch control) while completely abolishing all other intrinsic laryngeal function.
highYield: 4th arch (superior laryngeal nerve, external branch): thyroid cartilage + cricothyroid. 6th arch (RLN): all other intrinsic laryngeal muscles + arytenoid/cricoid cartilages. RLN injury spares cricothyroid.
pyqNote: The cricothyroid exception is extremely high-yield, tested in both anatomy and surgery (thyroid surgery RLN injury vs SLN injury). Fourth vs sixth arch is a direct PYQ.

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 60-year-old male presents with foot drop, sensory loss in the first web space of the foot, and weakness of toe extension but PRESERVED eversion. Which nerve branch is specifically injured?
explanation: The common peroneal nerve divides into the deep and superficial peroneal nerves. The DEEP peroneal nerve supplies: tibialis anterior (dorsiflexion), extensor digitorum longus (toe extension), extensor hallucis longus, and peroneus tertius. Its sensory distribution is limited to the first web space (dorsum between great and second toe). The SUPERFICIAL peroneal nerve supplies peroneus longus and brevis (EVERSION) and the dorsal foot skin (excluding first web space). In this case, eversion is PRESERVED (superficial peroneal intact), and foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion) + toe extension loss + first web space sensory loss = isolated deep peroneal nerve injury.
highYield: Deep peroneal nerve: dorsiflexion + toe extension + first web space sensation. Superficial peroneal nerve: eversion (peroneus longus/brevis) + dorsal foot sensation. Preserved eversion = deep peroneal nerve injury only.
pyqNote: The differentiation between deep vs superficial vs common peroneal injury based on eversion status is a high-yield discriminator frequently tested in NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient's oxygen consumption is measured at 250 mL/min. Arterial oxygen content is 200 mL/L and mixed venous oxygen content is 150 mL/L. What is the cardiac output calculated by the Fick principle?
explanation: Fick principle: CO = O2 consumption / (A-V O2 difference). CO = 250 mL/min ÷ (200 - 150) mL/L = 250 ÷ 50 = 5 L/min. This is the gold-standard method for measuring cardiac output, especially used in cardiac catheterization labs. Normal CO is 4.5–5.5 L/min.
highYield: Fick formula is a top-tested calculation in NEET PG. Always identify: O2 consumption (numerator) and A-V O2 difference (denominator).
pyqNote: Directly tested in NEET PG and AIIMS PG pattern questions involving cardiac catheterization scenarios.

subject: Physiology
stem: A healthy 25-year-old male has a heart rate of 72 beats/min and a stroke volume of 70 mL. What is his cardiac output, and which formula is used?
explanation: Cardiac Output (CO) = Stroke Volume (SV) × Heart Rate (HR). CO = 70 mL × 72 beats/min = 5,040 mL/min ≈ 5 L/min. Normal cardiac output is 4.5–5.5 L/min. Cardiac index = CO/BSA, normal 2.5–3.5 L/min/m².
highYield: The fundamental CO formula CO=SV×HR is foundational. Know normal values: CO 5 L/min, SV 70 mL, HR 72 bpm.
pyqNote: Appears as a basic recall or applied calculation in nearly every NEET PG physiology paper.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which of the following best describes the Frank-Starling law of the heart, and at what sarcomere length is stroke volume maximum?
options: Increased preload decreases stroke volume; maximum at 1.8 μm
Increased preload increases stroke volume up to an optimal sarcomere length of approximately 2.2 μm
Increased afterload increases stroke volume; maximum at 2.5 μm
Increased contractility is the same as increased preload; optimal at 2.0 μm
explanation: Frank-Starling law: as end-diastolic volume (preload) increases, stroke volume increases due to greater overlap of actin-myosin filaments, up to an optimal sarcomere length of ~2.2 μm. Beyond this, overlap decreases and SV falls. Preload = end-diastolic volume (EDV). This is the intrinsic autoregulation mechanism of the heart.
highYield: 2.2 μm sarcomere length for maximum force generation is a high-yield fact. Preload = EDV, not pressure alone.
pyqNote: Frank-Starling and optimal sarcomere length are classic NEET PG and USMLE Step 1 favorites.

subject: Physiology
stem: According to the law of Laplace, wall tension in a spherical ventricle is determined by which relationship?
options: Wall tension = Pressure × Radius / (2 × Wall thickness)
Wall tension = Pressure × Wall thickness / Radius
explanation: Laplace law for a sphere (ventricle): Wall Tension (T) = (Pressure × Radius) / (2 × Wall Thickness). This explains why a dilated heart (increased radius) requires more wall tension (oxygen demand) to generate the same pressure — the basis of cardiac failure pathophysiology. Hypertrophy (increased wall thickness) reduces wall tension, acting as a compensatory mechanism.
highYield: Laplace law explains ventricular hypertrophy as compensation for increased wall stress. Dilated cardiomyopathy = increased radius → increased wall tension → more O2 demand.
pyqNote: Laplace law formula and its clinical implications in heart failure are high-yield for NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: Thermodilution method for measuring cardiac output uses a Swan-Ganz catheter. Cold saline is injected into the right atrium and temperature change is measured in the pulmonary artery. If temperature change is SMALL, what does this indicate?
options: High cardiac output — blood dilutes cold saline rapidly
Low cardiac output — minimal dilution of cold saline
explanation: In thermodilution: CO is inversely proportional to the temperature change measured. A small temperature change = high cardiac output (cold saline is rapidly diluted and washed away by large blood flow). A large temperature change = low cardiac output (cold saline stays concentrated due to slow flow). CO ∝ 1/ΔT.
highYield: Thermodilution inverse relationship: small ΔT = high CO; large ΔT = low CO. Swan-Ganz catheter is placed in pulmonary artery.
pyqNote: Thermodilution method is frequently tested in NEET PG anesthesia/physiology overlap questions.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which of the following conditions is associated with HIGH cardiac output heart failure?
explanation: High-output cardiac failure occurs when the heart, though functioning normally or supra-normally, cannot meet excessive metabolic demands. Causes include: AV fistula, pregnancy, fever, hyperthyroidism, severe anemia, beriberi (thiamine deficiency), and Paget's disease. AV fistula causes shunting of blood directly from arterial to venous system, reducing SVR and increasing CO. Eventually, the heart fails under this sustained burden.
highYield: Mnemonic for high-output failure causes: 'APACHE' — Anemia, Pregnancy, AV fistula, Cirrhosis/beriberi, Hyperthyroidism, Exercise.
pyqNote: High-output heart failure causes are high-yield in NEET PG medicine and physiology.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient develops sudden bradycardia and hypotension after intracoronary injection of a chemical during angioplasty. Which reflex is being triggered?
explanation: The Bezold-Jarisch reflex is triggered by stimulation of ventricular mechanoreceptors (C-fiber afferents — J-receptors) by chemicals (serotonin, capsaicin, veratrum alkaloids) or excessive stretch. It results in a triad: bradycardia, vasodilation, and hypotension (cardioinhibitory + vasodepressor response). It is mediated via vagus nerve. It explains vasovagal syncope and the hypotension seen after inferior MI (inferior wall has high density of these receptors).
highYield: Bezold-Jarisch = bradycardia + vasodilation from ventricular C-fiber stimulation. Clinically relevant in inferior MI and vasovagal syncope.
pyqNote: AIIMS PG and NEET PG have tested this reflex in context of inferior MI bradycardia and angiography complications.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient with palpitations shows irregular cannon 'a' waves on JVP tracing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Cannon 'a' waves occur when the right atrium contracts against a closed tricuspid valve. In complete heart block, atria and ventricles contract independently (AV dissociation). Occasionally, the P wave (atrial contraction) coincides with a closed tricuspid valve (during ventricular systole), producing a giant 'a' wave — the cannon wave. Irregular cannon waves = complete heart block or VT with AV dissociation. Regular cannon waves = junctional rhythm or ventricular pacing.
highYield: Cannon a waves = atrium contracts against closed tricuspid = AV dissociation. Irregular cannon waves = complete heart block; regular = nodal rhythm.
pyqNote: JVP waveform interpretation including cannon waves is a high-frequency NEET PG topic.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which cardiac arrhythmia is characterized by the absence of 'a' wave in the JVP tracing?
explanation: The 'a' wave in JVP corresponds to atrial contraction. In atrial fibrillation (AF), there is no coordinated atrial contraction — only chaotic fibrillatory activity. Therefore, the 'a' wave is absent in AF. The JVP shows only c, v waves and x, y descents without distinct 'a' waves.
highYield: Absent 'a' wave = atrial fibrillation (no organized atrial contraction). This is a cornerstone JVP fact.
pyqNote: Classic one-liner tested repeatedly in NEET PG: 'Which condition shows absent a wave in JVP? — AF.'

subject: Physiology
stem: The 'v' wave in JVP is produced by which event?
options: Tricuspid valve closure and bulging into right atrium
Venous filling of the right atrium while the tricuspid valve is closed
Opening of the tricuspid valve with rapid ventricular filling
explanation: JVP wave components: 'a' wave = atrial contraction; 'c' wave = tricuspid valve bulging into RA during isovolumetric ventricular contraction; 'x' descent = atrial relaxation + downward displacement of AV ring; 'v' wave = venous filling of RA while tricuspid is still closed (during ventricular systole); 'y' descent = opening of tricuspid valve and rapid ventricular filling. The 'v' wave is exaggerated in tricuspid regurgitation.
highYield: Memorize: a=atrial contraction; c=tricuspid closure; x descent=atrial relaxation; v=venous filling; y descent=tricuspid opening.
pyqNote: JVP wave components are tested every year in NEET PG with either identification or clinical correlation.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient with a history of tuberculosis presents with raised JVP that increases on inspiration (Kussmaul sign) and rapid 'y' descent on JVP tracing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Constrictive pericarditis (common cause: TB in India) shows: (1) Kussmaul sign — JVP rises on inspiration because the stiff pericardium prevents increased venous return from being accommodated; (2) Steep/rapid 'y' descent — when tricuspid opens, blood rushes in rapidly then is abruptly halted by the rigid pericardium. Compare with cardiac tamponade: Kussmaul sign is absent (present only in tamponade in some cases), and 'x' descent is preserved but 'y' descent is blunted.
highYield: Constrictive pericarditis: Kussmaul sign + steep y descent + absent y in tamponade. TB most common cause in India.
pyqNote: Constrictive pericarditis vs tamponade JVP differences are among the highest-yield NEET PG topics in physiology and medicine.

subject: Physiology
stem: Steep 'y' descent in JVP is seen in all of the following EXCEPT:
explanation: Steep 'y' descent (rapid fall) is seen in: constrictive pericarditis (rapid inflow then abrupt halt), tricuspid regurgitation (large 'v' wave followed by rapid 'y' fall), ASD (high flow states). Right ventricular failure shows elevated JVP but the y descent is not characteristically steep — in fact, right heart failure causes slow y descent because the stiff non-compliant RV resists filling. Cardiac tamponade shows blunted/absent y descent.
highYield: Steep y descent: constrictive pericarditis, tricuspid regurgitation. Blunted y: cardiac tamponade. Slow y: RV failure.
pyqNote: Differential of y descent characteristics is a classic NEET PG negative-stem question.

subject: Physiology
stem: Inulin is considered the gold standard marker for measuring GFR. Why is inulin ideal for this purpose?
options: It is freely filtered and partially reabsorbed
It is freely filtered, not secreted, not reabsorbed, not synthesized, and not metabolized by the kidney
It is secreted by tubules, making it ideal for clearance measurement
It binds to plasma proteins, reflecting actual GFR
explanation: For a substance to be used to measure GFR accurately, it must be: freely filtered at the glomerulus, neither reabsorbed nor secreted by tubules, not synthesized/metabolized/stored in kidney. Inulin (a fructose polymer, MW ~5200 Da) fulfills all criteria. Its clearance directly equals GFR. Normal GFR = 125 mL/min. Creatinine is used clinically but overestimates GFR due to tubular secretion.
highYield: Gold standard GFR = inulin clearance. Clinical estimate = creatinine clearance (overestimates). Better marker = cystatin C.
pyqNote: Properties making inulin the gold standard for GFR measurement appear frequently in NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: Creatinine clearance overestimates GFR by approximately 10-20%. Which of the following explains this discrepancy?
options: Creatinine is partially reabsorbed by tubules
Creatinine is secreted by proximal tubules in addition to being filtered
Creatinine does not freely cross the glomerular membrane
Creatinine is synthesized within the kidney
explanation: Creatinine is freely filtered at the glomerulus AND a small but significant amount is actively secreted by the proximal tubules. This tubular secretion adds to the urinary creatinine beyond what was filtered, making the calculated clearance higher than the actual GFR. Hence creatinine clearance overestimates GFR. Cystatin C is a better marker as it is freely filtered, minimally secreted, and not affected by muscle mass.
highYield: Creatinine overestimates GFR due to tubular secretion. Cystatin C is the emerging superior marker not influenced by muscle mass or diet.
pyqNote: This concept appears in NEET PG as 'which marker overestimates/underestimates GFR' type questions.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient with acute kidney injury has urine Na 20 mEq/L, plasma Na 140 mEq/L, urine creatinine 120 mg/dL, and plasma creatinine 2 mg/dL. What is the FENa and what does it indicate?
explanation: FENa = (U[Na] × P[Cr]) / (P[Na] × U[Cr]) × 100 = (20 × 2) / (140 × 120) × 100 = 40/16800 × 100 = 0.238%. Wait — recalculating: = (20 × 2)/(140 × 120) × 100 = 40/16800 × 100 ≈ 0.24%. FENa <1% indicates pre-renal azotemia (tubules avidly reabsorb Na to conserve volume). FENa >2% indicates intrinsic renal failure (tubules damaged, cannot reabsorb Na). This patient's FENa <1% = pre-renal.
highYield: FENa formula: (UNa × PCr)/(PNa × UCr) × 100. <1% = pre-renal; >2% = intrinsic renal. Exception: FENa can be low in contrast nephropathy and myoglobinuria.
pyqNote: FENa calculation and interpretation is a high-frequency NEET PG nephrology-physiology question.

subject: Physiology
stem: A diabetic patient's plasma glucose is 250 mg/dL. What would you expect in the urine, and what is the transport maximum (Tm) for glucose reabsorption?
explanation: Renal threshold for glucose = 180 mg/dL. Above this plasma level, the filtered glucose load exceeds tubular reabsorptive capacity and glucosuria occurs. The transport maximum (Tm) for glucose is 375 mg/min (males) and slightly lower in females. SGLT2 transporters in the proximal tubule handle ~90% of glucose reabsorption. SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins) exploit this by blocking reabsorption, lowering plasma glucose.
highYield: Glucose threshold = 180 mg/dL. Tm = 375 mg/min. SGLT2 = 90% reabsorption in proximal tubule S1/S2 segments.
pyqNote: Glucose reabsorption threshold and Tm are tested in NEET PG physiology and pharmacology (SGLT2 inhibitors).

subject: Physiology
stem: ADH (vasopressin) acts on the collecting duct to concentrate urine. Which aquaporin channel does ADH specifically insert into the luminal membrane?
explanation: ADH (AVP) acts on V2 receptors on principal cells of the collecting duct. This activates adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylation of AQP2 water channels → insertion of AQP2 into the luminal (apical) membrane → increased water permeability → water reabsorption → concentrated urine. AQP3 and AQP4 are constitutively present on the basolateral membrane. AQP1 is in the proximal tubule and descending thin limb.
highYield: ADH inserts AQP2 into the apical (luminal) membrane of collecting duct principal cells. Nephrogenic DI = AQP2/V2 receptor defect.
pyqNote: AQP2 and ADH mechanism is repeatedly tested in NEET PG and AIIMS PG physiology.

subject: Physiology
stem: Aldosterone acts on the principal cells of the collecting duct via which mechanism to increase sodium reabsorption?
options: Binds to membrane receptor → rapid insertion of Na channels within seconds
Binds to mineralocorticoid receptor (nuclear) → protein synthesis → increased ENaC and Na/K-ATPase expression
Activates V2 receptor → cAMP → AQP2 insertion
Inhibits carbonic anhydrase → increased HCO3 reabsorption
explanation: Aldosterone is a steroid hormone. It crosses the cell membrane and binds to the cytoplasmic mineralocorticoid receptor (MR). The aldosterone-MR complex translocates to the nucleus → gene transcription → synthesis of proteins including ENaC (epithelial Na channel) on the apical membrane and Na/K-ATPase on the basolateral membrane. This increases Na+ reabsorption (and K+ secretion). The effect is delayed (hours) compared to peptide hormones. Principal cells handle Na+ and K+; intercalated cells handle acid-base.
highYield: Aldosterone = nuclear receptor → protein synthesis (genomic action). ENaC is the apical Na channel. Spironolactone blocks MR.
pyqNote: Aldosterone mechanism distinguishing genomic vs non-genomic action and ENaC is high-yield NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: Increased NaCl delivery to the macula densa causes which response to autoregulate GFR?
options: Afferent arteriolar constriction → decreased GFR (tubuloglomerular feedback)
explanation: Tubuloglomerular Feedback (TGF): When GFR increases, more NaCl reaches the macula densa (specialized cells in the thick ascending limb at the juxtaglomerular apparatus). The macula densa detects increased NaCl via NKCC2 cotransporter → releases ATP/adenosine → constricts the afferent arteriole → decreases glomerular capillary pressure → decreases GFR back to normal. Simultaneously, increased NaCl delivery inhibits renin release. This is the key intrinsic autoregulatory mechanism.
highYield: TGF: increased NaCl at macula densa → afferent arteriolar constriction → decreased GFR. Opposite of myogenic mechanism.
pyqNote: Tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism and direction of arteriolar change is tested in NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: Urea recycling in the renal medulla occurs mainly in which segment of the nephron, and this process is essential for what function?
options: Inner medullary collecting duct; generating and maintaining medullary hypertonicity for urine concentration
Thick ascending limb; diluting the tubular fluid
Cortical collecting duct; acidification of urine
explanation: Urea is passively absorbed from the inner medullary collecting duct (IMCD) via UT-A1 and UT-A3 urea transporters (regulated by ADH). This recycled urea accumulates in the medullary interstitium, contributing ~400 mOsm to the ~1200 mOsm maximum medullary osmolality (NaCl contributes ~600 mOsm). This high interstitial osmolality drives water reabsorption from the descending limb and concentrating duct, enabling production of concentrated urine. Urea recycling is essential for the countercurrent multiplier mechanism.
highYield: Urea recycling occurs in inner medullary collecting duct via UT-A1/UT-A3. ADH upregulates these transporters. Essential for medullary hypertonicity.
pyqNote: Urea recycling and medullary concentration mechanism are tested in NEET PG physiology renal concentration questions.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which of the following causes a RIGHT shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve?
explanation: Right shift of the O2-Hb dissociation curve = decreased affinity for O2 = more O2 released to tissues. Causes: increased 2,3-DPG, increased CO2 (Bohr effect), increased H+ (acidosis), increased temperature, and sickle cell anemia. Increased 2,3-DPG binds to beta chains of deoxyhemoglobin, stabilizing the T (tense/deoxy) state. Right shift helps tissues with high metabolic demand (exercise, fever) get more O2.
highYield: Right shift mnemonic: 'CADET face Right' — CO2, Acid (H+), 2,3-DPG, Exercise, Temperature (increased).
pyqNote: O2-Hb curve shifts are among the most tested physiology topics in NEET PG every year.

subject: Physiology
stem: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) has higher oxygen affinity than adult HbA. Which property of HbF explains this LEFT shift of the O2-Hb dissociation curve?
options: HbF has gamma chains instead of beta chains, which bind 2,3-DPG less avidly
HbF has delta chains that bind oxygen more tightly
HbF has an extra heme group increasing O2 binding
HbF is not subject to the Bohr effect
explanation: HbF has two alpha and two gamma chains (α2γ2), unlike HbA (α2β2). The gamma chains bind 2,3-DPG less avidly than beta chains. Since 2,3-DPG binding stabilizes the deoxy (T) state and causes right shift, less 2,3-DPG binding in HbF means it remains in the oxy (R) state more readily — resulting in higher O2 affinity (left shift). This allows fetal blood to extract O2 from maternal blood across the placenta (which has an intermediate PO2).
highYield: HbF left shift = gamma chains bind 2,3-DPG weakly → higher O2 affinity → extracts O2 from maternal hemoglobin at placenta.
pyqNote: HbF mechanism is a classic NEET PG fetal physiology question linked to the O2 dissociation curve.

subject: Physiology
stem: P50 is defined as the PO2 at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated with oxygen. What is the normal P50 value?
explanation: P50 is the PO2 at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated = 26 mmHg under standard conditions (pH 7.4, temp 37°C, PCO2 40 mmHg). A higher P50 = right-shifted curve = lower affinity = more O2 released. A lower P50 = left-shifted curve = higher affinity = less O2 released. P50 is a measure of hemoglobin's overall O2 affinity.
highYield: Normal P50 = 26 mmHg. Increased P50 = right shift. Decreased P50 = left shift. This is a direct NEET PG one-liner.
pyqNote: P50 value is directly asked as a one-liner in NEET PG physiology.

subject: Physiology
stem: The Bohr effect and the Haldane effect are considered reciprocal phenomena. Which statement correctly describes both?
options: Bohr effect: increased CO2 increases O2 affinity; Haldane effect: increased O2 increases CO2 carrying capacity
Bohr effect: increased CO2/H+ decreases O2 affinity (right shift); Haldane effect: oxygenation of Hb decreases its CO2 carrying capacity
Both effects describe the same phenomenon from the same perspective
Bohr effect is at tissues; Haldane effect is at alveoli — both increase CO2 transport
explanation: Bohr effect: Increased CO2/H+ at tissues → decreased O2 affinity of Hb (right shift) → more O2 delivered to tissues. Haldane effect: Oxygenation of Hb at lungs → Hb releases CO2 and H+ (deoxy-Hb carries more CO2 as carbaminohemoglobin and acts as better buffer). These are reciprocal: at tissues, Bohr effect facilitates O2 unloading while deoxy-Hb formed allows Haldane effect to load CO2. At lungs, the reverse occurs. Both enhance efficiency of gas exchange.
highYield: Bohr = CO2 affects O2 transport (reduced affinity). Haldane = O2 affects CO2 transport (deoxy-Hb carries more CO2). They are complementary.
pyqNote: Bohr-Haldane reciprocal relationship is a classic NEET PG conceptual question.

subject: Physiology
stem: Amino acids are reabsorbed in the proximal tubule by which transport mechanism?
options: Primary active transport directly using ATP
Secondary active cotransport driven by the Na+ electrochemical gradient
explanation: Amino acids are reabsorbed almost completely (>99%) in the proximal tubule by secondary active cotransport. Na+ enters the cell down its electrochemical gradient (maintained by basolateral Na/K-ATPase) and co-transports amino acids against their concentration gradient. This is similar to glucose reabsorption. Different carrier systems exist for neutral, acidic, basic, and imino acids. Failure of these carriers causes aminoaciduria (e.g., Hartnup disease for neutral amino acids, cystinuria for basic amino acids).
highYield: Amino acid reabsorption = secondary active cotransport with Na+. Defects: Hartnup (neutral AA), cystinuria (basic AA), Fanconi syndrome (all AA).
pyqNote: Amino acid transport mechanism and deficiency diseases are tested in NEET PG physiology and biochemistry.

subject: Physiology
stem: The 'x' descent in the JVP waveform is produced by which mechanism?
options: Atrial relaxation combined with downward displacement of the AV ring during ventricular systole
explanation: The 'x' descent has two components: (1) Atrial relaxation following the 'a' wave, and (2) Downward displacement of the AV ring (tricuspid annulus) as the ventricle contracts and shortens during systole. Both contribute to the fall in RA pressure seen as 'x' descent. The 'x' descent is exaggerated (prominent) in cardiac tamponade. In constrictive pericarditis, 'x' descent is preserved but 'y' descent is the dominant steep descent.
highYield: x descent = atrial relaxation + AV ring descent. Exaggerated x descent = cardiac tamponade. y descent = tricuspid opening.
pyqNote: Distinguishing x and y descent components is tested in NEET PG JVP interpretation questions.

subject: Physiology
stem: GFR is determined by Starling forces across the glomerular capillary and the filtration coefficient (Kf). Which combination of changes would DECREASE GFR?
options: Increased glomerular capillary pressure + decreased oncotic pressure
Decreased Kf + increased plasma oncotic pressure + decreased glomerular capillary pressure
Afferent arteriolar dilation + decreased oncotic pressure
Increased filtration surface area + decreased oncotic pressure
explanation: GFR = Kf × (Pgc - Pbs - πgc + πbs) where Pgc = glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure, Pbs = Bowman's space pressure, πgc = glomerular oncotic pressure, πbs = Bowman's space oncotic pressure (≈0). Factors decreasing GFR: (1) Decreased Kf (e.g., glomerulonephritis destroying filtration membrane), (2) Increased plasma oncotic pressure (dehydration), (3) Decreased Pgc (afferent constriction, heart failure), (4) Increased Pbs (ureteral obstruction). Option B combines all three decreasing factors.
highYield: GFR Starling forces: Pgc drives filtration; πgc and Pbs oppose it. Kf = hydraulic conductivity × surface area.
pyqNote: GFR determinants and Starling forces are analyzed in complex NEET PG and AIIMS PG physiology questions.

subject: Physiology
stem: Which of the following is a correct statement regarding myocardial contractility (inotropy)?
options: Contractility is represented by movement along a single Starling curve
Increased contractility shifts the Starling curve UPWARD AND TO THE LEFT, achieving greater SV at the same preload
Decreased preload is the same as decreased contractility
Contractility is solely determined by end-diastolic volume
explanation: Contractility (inotropy) refers to the intrinsic ability of the myocardium to contract independent of preload and afterload. On a Starling curve (SV vs EDV), changing preload moves ALONG the same curve. Increasing contractility (positive inotropy: catecholamines, digoxin, Ca2+) shifts the curve UPWARD AND LEFT — greater SV at the same EDV. Decreased contractility (heart failure, beta-blockers, ischemia) shifts the curve DOWNWARD AND RIGHT.
highYield: Preload = movement along curve. Contractility = shift of curve. Positive inotropy = upward-left shift. Negative = downward-right.
pyqNote: Starling curve shifts vs movements along the curve is a classic distinction tested in NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: Intercalated cells of the collecting duct are primarily responsible for which renal function?
options: Acid-base regulation via H+ secretion and HCO3- handling
explanation: The collecting duct has two cell types: (1) Principal cells — handle Na+ reabsorption (ENaC) and K+ secretion (ROMK), regulated by aldosterone; respond to ADH (AQP2). (2) Intercalated cells (Type A and B) — handle acid-base balance. Type A (alpha) intercalated cells: secrete H+ via H+-ATPase into lumen → HCO3- retained → acidify urine (active in acidosis). Type B (beta) intercalated cells: secrete HCO3- into lumen → H+ retained (active in alkalosis). Aldosterone activates Type A intercalated cells in addition to principal cells.
highYield: Principal cells: Na/K/water. Intercalated cells: H+/HCO3- (acid-base). Type A secretes H+; Type B secretes HCO3-.
pyqNote: Distinguishing principal vs intercalated cell functions is a direct NEET PG question.

subject: Physiology
stem: The 'c' wave in the jugular venous pulse is caused by:
options: Atrial contraction against a closed mitral valve
Bulging of the tricuspid valve into the right atrium during isovolumetric ventricular contraction
Rapid filling of the right ventricle
explanation: The 'c' wave in JVP occurs during isovolumetric ventricular contraction. As the ventricle begins to contract with both tricuspid and pulmonary valves closed, the tricuspid valve (which is under increasing ventricular pressure) bulges back (prolapses) into the right atrium, causing a small transient rise in RA pressure — seen as the 'c' wave. It is small and often not visible clinically. It follows the 'a' wave and precedes the 'x' descent.
highYield: c wave = tricuspid bulging into RA during isovolumetric contraction. Small wave, often not seen clinically. Between a wave and x descent.
pyqNote: JVP c-wave mechanism is tested in detailed JVP interpretation questions in NEET PG.

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient presents with cherry-red skin and headache after being in an enclosed space with a burning engine. Arterial PO2 is 95 mmHg but oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry reads 99%. What is the pathophysiology of impaired oxygen delivery in this case?
options: Decreased PO2 causes right shift of the O2-Hb curve
CO binds to hemoglobin forming COHb, left-shifts the O2-Hb curve and reduces O2-carrying capacity
MetHb formation causes right shift and increased O2 delivery
CO stimulates the Bezold-Jarisch reflex reducing cardiac output
